place_intro:
Bonifacio Global City, almost always called BGC, is a 240-hectare planned business and lifestyle district on the western side of Taguig, bordered by Makati to the west, EDSA to the north, and C5 to the east. The street grid follows a clear orthogonal plan, with named streets running north to south and numbered streets running east to west, which makes it walkable in a way most of Metro Manila is not.

The district is split into sub-areas locals navigate by name. Bonifacio High Street is the open-air retail spine between 7th and 9th Avenue. North of it is Uptown Bonifacio, the Megaworld cluster around Uptown Mall, Grand Hyatt Manila, and Mitsukoshi BGC. To the west are Burgos Circle and Forbes Town, quieter rotunda zones with cafes and brunch spots. On the eastern edge are Market! Market! and SM Aura Premier.

The crowd is a mix of office workers from PSE Tower and multinationals along 5th Avenue, students on field trips to the Mind Museum, expats around Forbes Town, and weekend visitors from across Metro Manila. It is built for cars but designed to be explored on foot, and locals tell DIY travelers to park once and walk between zones rather than drive across BGC.

history.heading: How BGC came to be
history.body:
The land BGC sits on was part of Fort William McKinley, a US Army installation established in 1902 on more than 2,500 hectares straddling Taguig, Makati, Pateros, and Pasig. The base was named after the 25th US president, William McKinley. The United States turned the property over to the Philippine government in 1949, and in 1957 it was renamed Fort Bonifacio after revolutionary leader Andres Bonifacio. It became the permanent headquarters of the Philippine Army.

The modern district began with the Bases Conversion and Development Authority, the agency created under the Ramos administration to convert former military camps into civilian use. In 1995, the BCDA sold a 440-hectare parcel of Fort Bonifacio, and Metro Pacific Corporation, then under First Pacific, formed Bonifacio Land Corporation to bid for and develop the property. Bonifacio Land entered a joint venture with BCDA called Fort Bonifacio Development Corporation, which became and remains the master planner of the district. The master plan was prepared by Ayala Land planners with consultants including W.B. Coscolluela and Associates and Parsons Brinckerhoff for traffic and civil engineering.

In 2003, the Ayala Corporation, through Ayala Land, and Evergreen Holdings of the Campos Group bought the controlling stake in Bonifacio Land from Metro Pacific, and the build-out accelerated. Bonifacio High Street opened in the mid-2000s as the first major retail spine. Megaworld Corporation later developed Forbes Town and Uptown Bonifacio. SM Aura Premier opened in 2013 as the eastern anchor, and the Philippine Stock Exchange moved its main office to PSE Tower at BGC in 2018.

place_intro:
SM Aura Premier sits at 26th Street corner McKinley Parkway in Bonifacio Global City, the business district built on the former Fort Bonifacio military reservation in Taguig. The mall holds down the southeastern edge of BGC, a short walk from Market! Market!, the Mind Museum, and the towers along 11th Avenue. Most visitors arrive by car, or by P2P bus from Ayala Center, Ortigas, and Alabang.

The mall reads as upscale rather than mass-market. Lower floors lean into international fashion and beauty brands. Upper floors hold the cinemas, Samsung Hall on the 6th level, and the Chapel of San Pedro Calungsod on the 7th. The 5th-floor Sky Park is the part most people remember, a landscaped rooftop with gardens, water features, sculptures, mini-golf, and open-air restaurants overlooking BGC.

Weekday foot traffic comes mostly from office workers in the SM Aura Tower and nearby BGC buildings. Weekends pull in families from Taguig and Pasig, plus expats from Serendra and One Bonifacio High Street. Taguig City Hall offices on the upper floors also bring in residents handling government transactions, giving the mall a different rhythm from a pure shopping center.

history.heading: How SM Aura came to be
history.body:
The land under SM Aura Premier was originally part of the Fort Bonifacio military reservation, the old Philippine Army base that the Bases Conversion and Development Authority began redeveloping in the 1990s into what is now Bonifacio Global City. In a 1999 memorandum of agreement, the BCDA committed to convey 5.1 hectares of Fort Bonifacio property to Taguig for a civic center. The Taguig city government later opened a 1.5-hectare portion of that civic center site to bidders for development.

SM Prime Holdings won the lease. The lease contracts were signed on November 21, 2007 and August 1, 2008, and a Deed of Conveyance dated January 17, 2008 transferred the property from the BCDA to Taguig City. The arrangement later became the subject of a public dispute between the BCDA and SM Prime over whether a private mall could stand on land originally meant for a civic center, with SM Prime maintaining that it was a lessee under a deal struck with the local government of Taguig.

Construction began in 2011. The mall was designed by Miami-based firm Arquitectonica, working with Filipino architect Willy Coscolluela and EDGE Interior Designers, on a narrow site that produced its distinctive angular, faceted facade. SM Aura Premier had its inaugural blessing on May 16, 2013 and opened to the public the following day, May 17, 2013. Samsung Hall, the 1,000-seat performance theater on the 6th level built in partnership with Samsung Electronics Philippines, followed on October 25, 2013. The 29-story SM Aura Office Tower, which also houses Taguig City Hall offices and several national government agencies, opened in September 2014.

place_intro:
SM Mall of Asia sits along Seaside Boulevard in Bay City, Pasay, on reclaimed land facing Manila Bay at the southern end of EDSA. The complex is part of the SM Central Business Park, which SM Prime later renamed the Mall of Asia Complex. EDSA basically dead-ends here, and the LRT-1 EDSA station plus carousel and provincial buses drop people within walking distance.

The mall itself splits into four interconnected buildings: the Main Mall, the Entertainment Mall that faces the bay, and the North and South Parking buildings. Around it sit the MOA Globe, the open-air SM By the Bay strip with the MOA Eye Ferris wheel, the SM Mall of Asia Arena, the SMX Convention Center, and the MOA Square expansion that houses IKEA Pasay City. Walkways and decks connect most of these, so visitors treat the complex as one loop.

You get a wide mix of people here. Families come on weekends for cinemas, arcades, and the bayside walk. Concert and event crowds gather around the Arena and SMX. Tourists staying near Roxas Boulevard or Conrad Manila drop by for the Manila Bay sunset. Office workers from the surrounding business park fill the food halls on weekday lunch breaks.

history.heading: How SM Mall of Asia came to be
history.body:
SM Mall of Asia opened on May 21, 2006, after a delayed construction schedule. SM Prime originally targeted a pre-Christmas 2005 opening, but supply issues and heavy rains pushed the launch first to March 2006 and then to May of that year. At the time of its opening, it was the largest shopping mall in the Philippines and one of the largest in Asia, a milestone tied directly to SM Prime founder Henry Sy Sr., who pushed for a flagship mall on the Manila Bay reclamation area.

The site itself is reclaimed land along Manila Bay, part of what was developed as the SM Central Business Park at the southern terminus of EDSA in Pasay. Before SM took it on, this stretch of Bay City was open reclaimed ground, tied to the wider Manila Bay reclamation work along the bay during the late 20th century. SM Prime built out its parcel as a mixed-use district with the mall as the centerpiece, surrounded by office buildings, the SMX Convention Center, and later the Mall of Asia Arena, which opened in 2012.

The master plan and mall design were led by Miami-based firm Arquitectonica, with Architect Robert Carag Ong as Architect of Record and GHT Services as project managers. Hilmarc's Construction Corp. and Monolith Construction Development Corp. handled construction. Arquitectonica also prepared the wider Bay City district plan, which is why the mall reads as part of a planned waterfront precinct rather than a standalone building. In 2016, SM Prime began a major expansion that added MOA Square and the IKEA Pasay City anchor, and the complex was rebranded the Mall of Asia Complex to reflect its shift from a single mall into a broader business and lifestyle district.

place_intro:
Greenbelt is the lifestyle wing of Ayala Center in Makati, sitting along Paseo de Roxas, Esperanza, Legazpi, and Makati Avenue. It is part of the Makati Central Business District, the same block group that holds Glorietta, Landmark, and the Ayala Triangle Gardens. Office workers from the surrounding towers enter from the Landmark side, while visitors often come in from Greenbelt 5 along Esperanza.

The complex has five connected wings labeled Greenbelt 1 to 5, wrapped around an open garden park with koi ponds, a lagoon, and the round Sto. Nino de Paz Chapel at the center. Greenbelt 3 and 4 hold the international labels and the cinemas. Greenbelt 5 leans into luxury houses and Filipino designer boutiques. Greenbelt 1, the oldest wing, is closed for redevelopment. The garden in the middle is what most locals just call "Greenbelt," where families, couples, churchgoers, and salarymen on coffee breaks all cross paths.

People come here for cinema dates, Sunday Mass at the open chapel, after-work drinks at the Greenbelt 2 and 3 strips, and shopping that runs from streetwear to luxury. It functions less like one mall and more like a small district inside Ayala Center.

history.heading: How Greenbelt in Ayala Center came to be
history.body:
The land Greenbelt sits on is part of the old Hacienda San Pedro de Macati, the estate the Zobel de Ayala family began developing into the Makati Central Business District in the 1950s. The Greenbelt area itself started not as a mall but as a public park inside the growing Ayala Center, with low-rise dining pavilions, an aviary, and an open lawn that locals called Greenbelt Junction during the 1970s.

The central landmark of that early park, the Santo Nino de Paz Chapel, was inaugurated on July 28, 1983. It was blessed by Cardinal Jaime Sin, conceptualized as a thanksgiving vow by Fanny Diploma, supported by the Ayala landowners led by Jaime Zobel de Ayala, and designed by architects William Fernandez and Jess Dizon, with later glass works by Ramon Orlina. The round open-air chapel still anchors the park today.

The park, surrounding pavilions, and open parking were consolidated and reopened as the retail complex now called Greenbelt in 1988, developed by Ayala Land as the company's second mall after Quad. Ayala Land positioned it as the country's first lifestyle center, integrating shopping, dining, a chapel, and a garden in one block. National Artist for Architecture Leandro Locsin was involved in the original Greenbelt 1 wing.

The modern multi-wing Greenbelt took shape in the 2000s. Greenbelt 2 and 3 broke ground in 2000 and opened in 2002, Greenbelt 4 opened in 2004, and Greenbelt 5 opened in 2007. The master plan that turned Greenbelt Park into the centerpiece was prepared by Edward D. Stone Jr. and Associates, with the new wings designed by Callison and GF and Partners. In 2024, Ayala Malls closed Greenbelt 1 for a redevelopment led by San Francisco firm Gensler.
history.citation_label: Wikipedia, Ayala Land, Urban Land Institute

place_intro:
Power Plant Mall sits inside Rockwell Center, a mixed-use estate straddling the Makati barangays of Poblacion and Guadalupe Viejo. The estate is wedged between Estrella Drive on the north and the Pasig River on the south, with J.P. Rizal extension feeding traffic in from EDSA. The mall sits at the center, surrounded by condo towers like the Rizal, Luna, and Amorsolo residences, the Edades Tower, and offices such as PHINMA Plaza and 8 Rockwell.

The mall is compact, laid out across four retail levels around an open atrium. The R1 to R4 floors hold mid to high-end fashion, beauty, and lifestyle tenants, with a cinema cluster and supermarket on the lower levels. Outside, the grounds connect to the Rockwell Tent for trade fairs and concerts and a covered promenade linking the towers to the retail block. Foot traffic skews to Rockwell residents, Ateneo Graduate School of Business students, and Makati office workers.

People come here for a quieter alternative to Greenbelt or BGC. The estate is private, well guarded, and walkable end to end in about fifteen minutes. Weekdays feel residential. Weekends pull in a wider Metro Manila crowd for the cinema and Rockwell Tent events.

history.heading: How Rockwell Center came to be
history.body:
The land under Power Plant Mall used to hold the Rockwell Thermal Plant, a 130-megawatt facility built and operated by Manila Electric Railroad and Light Company, the Lopez-led predecessor of Meralco. The plant was named after James Rockwell, an early president of the company. It ran from 1950 until December 14, 1973, when a fire shut it down. The site stayed idle for two decades while the Lopez group considered reopening it.

In 1994, after Makati residents pushed back against restarting the thermal plant, the Lopezes decommissioned the facility for good. The following year, in 1995, the family set up Rockwell Land Corporation to redevelop the property into a mixed-use district. It was the first major Makati estate built outside the Ayala family's holdings, which had until then dominated the city's commercial core.

Master planning went to Filipino architect Felino Palafox and his firm, Palafox Associates, while the global firm Skidmore, Owings and Merrill handled architectural design under partner Larry Oltmanns. Construction of the estate began in 1998. The Power Plant Mall, the retail centerpiece of the development, opened on December 26, 2000. Residential towers, office buildings, and the Ateneo Graduate School of Business campus followed, and Rockwell Land began a fresh round of expansion from 2012 onward, adding the Edades Tower, the Proscenium complex, and more office space.

place_intro:
SM Megamall sits along EDSA corner Julia Vargas Avenue in Ortigas Center, Mandaluyong. It is one of the main anchors of the Ortigas business district, with the Ortigas MRT station and Shaw Boulevard MRT station both within walking distance. The complex stretches over roughly 10 hectares, split into Buildings A, B, the Mega Atrium link, the Building C transport terminal and office block, and the Mega Fashion Hall on the Julia Vargas side.

Locals usually call the two main wings Mega A and Mega B, connected by the Mega Atrium since 2008. Mega A leans toward department store, supermarket, and family shopping floors, while Mega B is the cinema, electronics, and hobby side. The Mega Fashion Hall, opened in 2014, holds the bigger international fashion brands, higher-end cinemas, and an al fresco dining strip on the upper level.

The crowd is a mix of Ortigas office workers on weekdays, families from Mandaluyong, Pasig, and San Juan on weekends, and MRT commuters cutting through the mall. Most visitors pick one building to focus on per trip because of the size. Building C also works as a PUV terminal, so the property doubles as a transport node for the eastern side of Metro Manila.

history.heading: How SM Megamall came to be
history.body:
SM Megamall opened on June 28, 1991, developed by Henry Sy Sr. and the SM group through First Asia Realty, with SM Prime Holdings as the long-term operator. Construction started in 1989 on a roughly 10-hectare site at the EDSA and Julia Vargas Avenue corner of Ortigas Center, Mandaluyong. According to Wikipedia and SM company materials, the project was funded partly through a one-billion-peso loan from Philippine National Bank and Citibank, which was a major figure for a single retail project at the time.

It was the third SM supermall after SM City North EDSA in 1985 and SM City Sta. Mesa in 1986, and on opening it became the largest shopping center in the Philippines, with an initial gross leasable area cited at around 331,680 square meters. The original architecture was credited to Antonio Sindiong together with the Miami-based firm Arquitectonica, which gave the building its distinct curved facade along EDSA.

The complex grew in stages. The Mega Atrium, a roughly 16,000 square meter link between Buildings A and B, was added in 2008 and was designed by W.V. Coscolluela and Associates. Building C, an 11-story mixed-use block with offices, parking, and a public transport terminal, followed in 2011. The Mega Fashion Hall, also called Building D, opened in 2014 and added around 86,000 square meters of retail, helping the mall reclaim the title of largest in the country at the time. In June 2024, SM Prime announced a longer-term redesign of the property with UK firm Benoy, the same group behind ION Orchard and Jewel Changi Airport, with rollout planned over the following years.

place_intro:
SM City North EDSA sits on the corner of North Avenue and EDSA in Quezon City, where the MRT-3 North Avenue Station meets the bus terminals along EDSA. It is generally treated as the largest mall in the Philippines by gross floor area, so regulars navigate it as a cluster of connected buildings. The main parts locals reference are the Main Mall (City Center), The Annex, The Block, the Northlink, and the open-air Sky Garden on the upper deck.

The crowd is a wide mix because the location pulls from across the north. You get UP Diliman and Ateneo students on the train, families from Fairview doing grocery runs at the Hypermarket, BPO workers from Northlink, and provincial travelers from the EDSA terminals. The Block leans younger, with cinemas, Toy Kingdom, and casual dining. The Annex holds Uniqlo, the food court, and bowling lanes. Sky Garden is the unofficial meet-up spot, anchored by the Sky Dome.

For DIY visitors, the easiest landmark is the Grass Residences condo towers rising above the mall. From the MRT station you can walk straight into the basement of the Annex. Parking spreads across the Car Park Plaza and Interior Zone, with covered footbridges linking everything.

history.heading: How SM North EDSA came to be
history.body:
The land SM City North EDSA stands on has a layered backstory. Under the 1941 Frost Plan for Quezon City, the 46-hectare site was designated as the National Exposition Grounds for the new capital. Through the 1970s, parts of it were used as a U.S. Navy Transmitter Station Naval Reservation, and a portion was held by the Government Service Insurance System with plans to build housing for public school teachers. None of those projects fully materialized, and by the early 1980s the corner of North Avenue and EDSA was still mostly marshland.

Henry Sy Sr., who had grown SM from a single shoe store on Carriedo into a department store chain, acquired roughly 16 hectares of that marshland and broke ground in 1983. The timing looked risky because the country was deep in political crisis after the Aquino assassination and interest rates were climbing past 40 percent. The mall opened on November 8, 1985, originally branded as The SM Center North EDSA. It was the first SM Supermall and the project that introduced the integrated mall format, anchored by an SM Department Store and SM Supermarket under one roof, to the Philippine market.

Expansions followed in stages. The Block, also called Annex 3, opened on July 28, 2006 with a hypermarket, more cinemas, and the flagship Toy Kingdom. A new Annex building with its curved aquamarine facade opened in December 2008. In May 2009, the Sky Garden opened as a quarter-mile linear park on the upper deck, with the 1,500-seat Sky Dome at one end. The six-story Northlink, housing BPO offices above retail, was completed in 2011. Grass Residences condominium towers later rose on the same superblock, turning the site into a mixed-use district.

place_intro:
Glorietta sits at the core of Ayala Center in Makati, the central business district of Metro Manila. The complex is bounded by Ayala Avenue, Makati Avenue, Pasay Road, and EDSA, and connects on foot to Greenbelt, Landmark, and SM Makati. The Palm Drive entrance is the usual drop-off for taxis and Grab, while MRT-3 Ayala Station is a short covered walk away.

The mall has five sections. Glorietta 1, 2, 3, and 4 are joined as one contiguous building with up to five retail levels, while Glorietta 5 is a separate structure with three retail floors and BPO offices above. At the center of Glorietta 1 and 2 is the Palm Drive Activity Center, the open atrium where Ayala holds product launches, concerts, and the annual Christmas tree lighting. The interior is maze-like, so first-timers orient by the activity center or the Glorietta 4 cinema lobby.

On weekdays the foot traffic is mostly Makati office workers from the surrounding corporate towers. On weekends, families from across Metro Manila come in for shopping, movies at Ayala Malls Cinemas, and events at the activity center. Tourists at Dusit Thani or The Peninsula use Glorietta as their base for pasalubong runs.

history.heading: How Glorietta grew out of the old Makati Commercial Center
history.body:
Before Glorietta, the site was the heart of the Makati Commercial Center, the pioneering shopping district that Ayala Corporation opened in the 1960s as part of the master plan that turned the former Hacienda de San Pedro de Macati into Metro Manila's premier business district. At the middle of the complex was the Glorietta park itself, an open landscaped area with an outdoor stage built in the 1970s. National Artist for Architecture Ildefonso P. Santos Jr. is credited with the landscape work on the park. Around it stood Makati Supermart, Sulo Restaurant, and small shopping arcades including Mayfair Center, Bricktown, and The Quad, a four-cinema arcade that lent its name to the first version of the mall.

In 1990, Ayala decided to redevelop the Makati Commercial Center and merge it with the adjacent Greenbelt into a single rebranded district called Ayala Center, which took effect in 1991. The new mall opened in 1991 with a gross leasable area of around 250,000 square meters, initially branded as Quad. Quad 1 retained the original four cinemas, Quad 2 rose on the former Makati Supermart site, and Quad 3 replaced the old Sulo Restaurant lot. The complex was officially renamed Glorietta in 1997.

Glorietta 4 followed in 1998, with construction tied to the rise of the Oakwood Premier tower above it. After a 2007 explosion damaged Glorietta 2, Ayala Land reconstructed Glorietta 1 and 2, reopening them on November 5 and December 7, 2012, together with the current Palm Drive Activity Center. Glorietta 5, the detached wing with BPO offices on top, opened in 2018. The complex is owned by Ayala Land and operated by its retail arm, Ayala Malls.

place_intro:
TriNoma sits on the east side of EDSA at North Avenue in Quezon City, right beside the MRT-3 North Avenue Station and the Line 3 depot it was built over. The name is a syllabic shortening of "Triangle North of Manila." It is an Ayala Malls property and is often compared to SM North EDSA, which faces it across EDSA.

The mall is a six-storey complex with about 130,000 square meters of retail space arranged around a round central atrium, with wings radiating out from it. Locals know it by parts: the Mindanao Lobby, the Activity Center where events happen, the al fresco zones with water features, and the rooftop garden park on top of the building. The North Avenue Wing is being expanded into The Exchange at TriNoma, a mixed-use extension that will link the mall to the Unified Grand Central Station.

The crowd is mostly Quezon City residents from surrounding barangays, students from nearby universities, and commuters who pass through North Avenue station every day. Weekends bring in families from the north by car, while weekday afternoons fill with office workers from the Vertis North side and transit riders making a quick stop before catching the train, jeep, or P2P bus home.

history.heading: How TriNoma came to be
history.body:
Before TriNoma stood on the lot, the site was known as People's Park, a flea market set up by then Quezon City Mayor Brigido R. Simon Jr. as a livelihood project for informal settlers in the area. The market was closed in 1995 under Mayor Ismael A. Mathay Jr. to clear the ground for the MRT-3 depot that would service the line running along EDSA. From December 1999 to December 2004, the depot land next to the tracks sat largely idle.

The commercial development moved forward through the North Triangle Depot Commercial Corporation, a joint vehicle that held the lease from the Department of Transportation and Communications. Ayala Land bought out the Fil-Estate Group's 30.89 percent stake in the corporation for 600.1 million pesos, consolidating control of the project. Construction began in June 2005 under the working name North Triangle Commercial Center, later rebranded TriNoma, a name inspired by the TriBeCa redevelopment in New York and built on the Triangle North of Manila shorthand.

The mall was designed by GF & Partners Architects, the same firm behind other Ayala Malls properties, with a concept built around curved corridors, an open central atrium, al fresco zones, and a rooftop garden park. TriNoma was inaugurated in a private ceremony on April 25, 2007, attended by Ayala Land officials and then Quezon City Mayor Feliciano Belmonte Jr. The soft opening was pushed from May 3 due to unfinished structural elements, and the mall opened to the public on May 16, 2007. It was Ayala Malls' first major project north of Metro Manila proper.

place_intro:
Eastwood City sits on an 18.5-hectare block in Barangay Bagumbayan, Libis, Quezon City, fronting the E. Rodriguez Jr. Avenue segment of C-5. It is a self-contained township where you park once and walk to everything. The core is split into two sides facing each other: Eastwood Mall on one end and Eastwood Citywalk 1 and 2 on the other, linked by a central plaza.

The crowd shifts by the hour. During the day it leans corporate because of the office towers inside the PEZA-accredited cyberpark, so BPO workers fill the food halls at lunch and after their shifts. By late afternoon, students and residents from the 23 condominium towers take over the plaza. After 10 PM, Citywalk becomes the active side, with its open-air strip of bars drawing young professionals from across Metro Manila.

The layout is walkable end to end in about fifteen minutes. The Mall side groups the air-conditioned retail, cinemas, and sit-down restaurants. The Citywalk side runs as an open-air promenade lined with casual dining, karaoke spots, and clubs. Between them are the central fountain, a chapel, and the residential lobbies.

history.heading: How Eastwood City came to be
history.body:
Before Eastwood City, the Libis-Bagumbayan stretch between C-5 and the Marikina River was a working industrial belt. The land hosted factories and warehouses for companies like Mercury Drug and Alaska Milk, and it sat outside the usual commercial map of Quezon City. Megaworld Corporation, led by Andrew Tan, acquired a piece of that industrial complex in the mid-1990s with a different plan for the site.

Megaworld launched Eastwood City in 1997 as the company's first township and the first project of its kind in the country, built around a live-work-play concept. The same year, the Philippine Economic Zone Authority designated the development as the country's first IT park, or cyberpark, which gave locator companies tax incentives for IT and business process outsourcing operations. That accreditation pulled the early wave of BPO firms into Libis and helped seed the industry that later expanded to Ortigas, Makati, and BGC.

The original 18.5-hectare master plan covered residential condominiums, office towers, a hotel, and the lifestyle mall component. Eastwood Mall and Citywalk were rolled out in phases through the 2000s, with Eastwood Mall opening in 2007 as the larger enclosed retail anchor. By the time the township matured, it counted 23 residential towers, 11 office buildings, and three lifestyle malls inside the same block.

In June 2025, Megaworld announced a 2.5 billion peso redevelopment program for Eastwood City aimed at refreshing the public spaces, upgrading the malls, and modernizing the township after nearly three decades of operation.

place_intro:
Shangri-La Plaza sits at the corner of EDSA and Shaw Boulevard in Mandaluyong, at the southern edge of Ortigas Center. It is one of four large malls around the Shaw intersection, sharing the area with SM Megamall across the street, Robinsons Galleria to the north, and The Podium deeper into Ortigas. The mall connects directly to the Shaw Boulevard MRT-3 station via a covered footbridge.

The complex is split into the Main Wing, which faces EDSA and uses Rustan's as its anchor, and the East Wing, which opened later and leans more upscale. Regulars point to specific zones: the Food Forum and The Marketplace grocery in the basement, The Fifth on the fifth level, The Ledge for sit-down restaurants, the Red Carpet cinemas upstairs, and the Streetscape al fresco strip on the East Wing side.

The crowd skews office workers from the Ortigas business district on weekdays, families and condo residents from One Shangri-La Place and St. Francis Shangri-La Place on weekends, and a steady flow of mall walkers and churchgoers heading to the St. Padre Pio Chapel on the sixth floor. Locals treat it as the calmer, more polished option compared to Megamall next door.

history.heading: How Shangri-La Plaza came to be
history.body:
Shangri-La Plaza opened to the public on November 21, 1991, at the corner of EDSA and Shaw Boulevard in Mandaluyong. It was developed by EDSA Properties Holdings Inc. together with the Kuok Group of Companies, the Malaysian conglomerate led by Robert Kuok that also owns the worldwide chain of Shangri-La Hotels and Resorts. Construction of the seven-story structure ran from 1989 to 1991, and the mall opened roughly five months after SM Megamall began operating across the avenue.

At the time it opened, the upscale shopping mall format was still new in Metro Manila. The closest reference point for Filipino shoppers was the Rustan's department store, which was a single store rather than a full mall, so Shangri-La Plaza was positioned as the country's first attempt at a luxury oriented retail complex. The original design featured a 25-meter high atrium ceiling, revolving glass doors, and express escalators, details that were uncommon in Philippine malls of that era. Rustan's eventually took the four-level anchor space inside the Main Wing.

The Kuok Group expanded the property in stages. Shangri-La's EDSA Plaza Hotel, later renamed Edsa Shangri-La, Manila, opened next to the mall in 1992. The St. Francis Shangri-La Place residential towers followed in the 2000s, and the East Wing of the mall, together with the One Shangri-La Place condominium towers, was completed and opened in 2013. The mall is operated today under Shang Properties, Inc., the Philippine listed arm of the Kuok Group.

place_intro:
Solaire Resort and Casino sits at 1 Aseana Avenue in the Tambo district of Parañaque, on the southern edge of Metro Manila. The property covers around 8.3 hectares inside Entertainment City, the 8 square kilometer gaming zone PAGCOR carved out along Roxas Boulevard. Solaire was the first integrated resort to open in this strip, with City of Dreams Manila and Okada Manila later filling the same reclaimed waterfront block.

The complex splits into two wings. The Bay Tower faces Manila Bay and opened first, holding 488 rooms, the main casino floor, The Shoppes retail arcade, and the casual dining row. The Sky Tower opened later as the all suite premium wing with 312 rooms, a pool deck, and The Theatre at Solaire, a 1,760 seat venue for touring Broadway shows and concerts.

Visitors are a mix of international gaming guests, domestic weekenders using it as a staycation spot, and Parañaque locals who drop in for the cinema or Sunday buffet. NAIA Terminal 1 is about a ten minute drive via the NAIA Expressway, and SM Mall of Asia sits north along Macapagal Boulevard. Most guests arrive by private car or hotel shuttle.

history.heading: How Solaire and Entertainment City came to be
history.body:
The land Solaire occupies is part of a 120 hectare reclaimed parcel along Manila Bay, designated by the Philippine Economic Zone Authority as a special economic zone for tourism and gaming. The wider master plan dates back to 2002, when PAGCOR first envisioned Entertainment City as a Philippine answer to the integrated resort strips of Macau and Singapore. The zone, formally called Bagong Nayong Pilipino Entertainment City, was set aside along the western side of Roxas Boulevard in barangay Tambo, Parañaque, with PAGCOR acting as both regulator and land lessor.

Bloomberry Resorts Corporation, the gaming arm controlled by port magnate Enrique K. Razon Jr., won one of the four PAGCOR licenses and committed 1.2 billion US dollars to the project through its operating subsidiary Sureste Properties. The master plan and architecture were handled by Paul Steelman of Las Vegas based Steelman Partners, with interior work by DSAA and lighting by shop12, all firms with prior credits on Strip and Cotai casino projects.

Solaire opened to the public on March 16, 2013, becoming the first of the Entertainment City integrated resorts to start operating. The Sky Tower expansion followed later, adding the all suite wing and The Theatre at Solaire. City of Dreams Manila opened next door on February 2, 2015, and Okada Manila joined the strip on December 30, 2016, completing the original PAGCOR trio that surrounds Solaire today.

In 2024 Bloomberry opened a second property, Solaire Resort North, inside the Vertis North development in Quezon City, making the Parañaque flagship the southern anchor of a two property local portfolio. A third Solaire is planned for Puerto Azul in Cavite, targeted for 2028.

place_intro:
Okada Manila sits on a 44-hectare property inside Entertainment City, the gaming and integrated resort strip along the Manila Bay reclamation in Parañaque. The address is on New Seaside Drive, off the coastal road connecting NAIA to Macapagal Boulevard. Most guests arrive via Uber or Grab from the airport in about 15 to 25 minutes. Its neighbors are Solaire to the south and City of Dreams Manila across the road.

The complex is laid out around a few clear anchors. The Crystal Corridor and Crystal Pavilion ring the property like a glass arcade and lead to the Retail Boulevard, where boutiques and cafes sit. At the center is The Fountain, the dancing water feature that runs scheduled shows every evening. Toward the bay side is Cove Manila, the glass domed pool, beach club, and nightclub, while the main gaming floors and hotel lobbies face the porte cochere.

The crowd mixes local staycationers, families using the pools and PLAY Kids Club, Chinese, Korean, and Japanese tour groups, and gaming guests who barely leave the casino floor. Daytime feels quiet around the pools and spa, while the Fountain plaza, Cove, and buffet halls fill up from late afternoon onward.

history.heading: How Okada Manila came to be
history.body:
The Okada Manila site is part of Entertainment City, the integrated resort district that the Philippine Amusement and Gaming Corporation (PAGCOR) first laid out in 2002 on reclaimed land along Manila Bay. The reclamation itself was carried out by the national government through the 1970s and 1980s, originally under the Bagong Nayong Pilipino plan, before PAGCOR repositioned the parcels as a casino and tourism zone. Four casino licenses were eventually awarded for the strip that now hosts Solaire, City of Dreams Manila, Okada Manila, and the long delayed Westside City project.

The Okada parcel was secured by Tiger Resort, Leisure and Entertainment, Inc., a Philippine subsidiary of Japan's Universal Entertainment Corporation. Universal Entertainment, formerly Aruze Corporation, was founded in 1969 by Japanese businessman Kazuo Okada, originally as a pachinko and slot machine maker. Construction of the Manila property began in 2007 and ran for roughly seven years at a reported cost of about 250 billion yen. The architect of record was Pimentel Rodriguez Simbulan and Partners (PRSP) of the Philippines, working with Miurashin Architect and Associates of Japan as design consultant.

The project was first marketed as Manila Bay Resorts, then rebranded as Okada Manila in June 2016. It opened in preview mode on December 21, 2016, with the casino formally starting operations on December 30, 2016. Kazuo Okada was later removed as chairman of Tiger Resort in 2017 after Universal Entertainment alleged misuse of funds, though Tiger Resort remains the licensed operator under PAGCOR.

place_intro:
Conrad Manila sits along Seaside Boulevard inside the Mall of Asia Complex in Pasay, on the reclaimed Bay City strip facing Manila Bay. The hotel occupies the top eight floors of a curved glass building, while the lower two floors hold S Maison, an upscale retail wing connected to the property. From the lobby it is a short walk to the SM Mall of Asia main mall, and the MOA Arena stands about two blocks away, which makes the hotel a common pick for guests attending concerts and conventions.

The building, designed by Singapore firm WOW Architects, takes its cue from yachts and cruise ships crossing Manila Bay, so it reads as a long sail from Seaside Boulevard. The 347 rooms and suites sit above a podium that holds the ballroom, function rooms, the pool deck, and the spa. Rooms face either the bay side or the city side toward Entertainment City in Parañaque.

The guest mix skews business travelers using the MICE facilities, families parking near MOA for weekend trips, and inbound flyers who want a short transfer from NAIA. The walking radius covers S Maison, the SMX Convention Center, One Esplanade, and the seaside promenade fronting Manila Bay.

history.heading: How Conrad Manila came to be
history.body:
The land under Conrad Manila is part of the Bay City reclamation, a project formerly called Boulevard 2000 that was initiated under First Lady Imelda Marcos in 1977 through what is now the Philippine Reclamation Authority. By the time the Marcos administration ended in early 1986, around 660 hectares had been reclaimed from Manila Bay, including the Cultural Center of the Philippines Complex. Decades later, SM Prime tied its Mall of Asia development to this same reclaimed strip, and the Conrad site sits on the seaward edge of that 42 hectare master plan.

The Conrad project was announced on March 7, 2013, when SM Hotels and Conventions Corporation, a subsidiary of SM Investments Corporation, signed a management agreement with Hilton Worldwide for the Conrad Hotels and Resorts brand. The hotel was first targeted to open in mid 2015 with around 350 rooms, but construction ran past that timeline. Reported construction cost for the building was about 6.5 billion pesos.

Conrad Manila opened on June 15, 2016 with a ribbon cutting ceremony, and the formal launch followed in September 2016. It was the first Conrad branded property in the Philippines. The building was designed by WOW Architects of Singapore, with the form referencing yachts and cruise ships on Manila Bay. The S Maison retail wing on the lower floors opened to the public in June 2017, with interiors by French design firm Malherbe themed around the Pearl of the Orient concept. Ownership remains with SM Hotels and Conventions Corporation, while operations are run by Hilton.

place_intro:
Nustar Resort and Casino sits on Kawit Point at the western end of the South Road Properties (SRP) in Cebu City, a reclaimed peninsula that juts into the Mactan Channel. The complex covers around 9.7 hectares and is the first integrated resort in the Visayas, so the layout follows the casino-plus-hotels template you see in Manila Bay rather than the beach format common elsewhere in Cebu. Drivers reach it via SRP Road from downtown, or from the Cebu-Cordova Link Expressway.

Inside the property, the parts most visitors deal with are the casino floor, the Nustar Mall retail strip, the Grand Ballroom and convention rooms, and three hotel components: Fili Hotel, which opened first with 379 rooms, the ultra-luxury Nustar Hotel with sea-facing villas, and a third hotel block on the masterplan. There is also a waterfront promenade and the Nustar Theater.

The crowd skews toward weekend day-trippers from Cebu, Filipinos flying in for staycations, foreign gaming guests, and MICE delegates. DIY travelers usually pair a Nustar stop with a CCLEX drive or a visit to SM Seaside across the SRP.

history.heading: The origins of Nustar Resort and Casino
history.body:
The land Nustar stands on did not exist as usable property until the South Road Properties reclamation closed off a stretch of the Mactan Channel. The SRP is a 300-hectare reclamation off the southern coast of Cebu City, financed by a 12.315 billion yen loan from the Japan Bank for International Cooperation. Civil works started in 1997 and were completed in 2004, which turned the former Kawit Island and the shallows around it into a contiguous waterfront strip the city government could lease out.

The Nustar project itself is a joint venture between the Cebu City Government and Universal Hotels and Resorts, Inc. (UHRI), an affiliate of JG Summit Holdings under the Gokongwei group. The joint venture agreement was signed on August 17, 2018 between then-mayor Tomas Osmena and UHRI president Frederick Go, and ground was broken eight days later, on August 25, 2018, on the Kawit Point parcel at the western tip of the SRP.

Construction ran through the pandemic years, and Nustar opened in phases starting in 2022, with the casino, Fili Hotel, the mall, the convention center, and the theater coming online first. The concept architecture for the integrated resort was done by HKS, with interiors by Hirsch Bedner Associates, PIA Interior, and local firm ASYA Design. The ultra-luxury Nustar Hotel tower was added later in the rollout, making Nustar the first integrated resort in Cebu and the wider southern Philippines.

place_intro:
Bonifacio High Street is an open-air retail strip running about one kilometer along 5th Avenue inside Bonifacio Global City, the central business district of Taguig. It sits roughly between 7th and 11th Avenues, laid out as four blocks with a long landscaped park down the middle and shops, restaurants, and cafes on both sides. The main avenue is closed to vehicles, so people walk the length on wide sidewalks.

The strip has clear sections. Blocks 1 and 2 are the original open-air retail rows. Further along is High Street Central, a mix of open-air walkways and indoor buildings with the cinemas and bigger anchor stores. Central Square is the indoor luxury wing run by SSI Group, and One Bonifacio High Street is the newer end near the Philippine Stock Exchange and Shangri-La at The Fort.

The crowd is a mix of BGC office workers on lunch, families on weekend walks, expats from nearby condos, students from the international schools, and out-of-towners spending a day around The Mind Museum. Weekends bring pop-up markets and events on the central lawn. It is flat and walkable, a common meetup point before people head to Uptown, Burgos Circle, or Serendra.

history.heading: How Bonifacio High Street came to be
history.body:
The land under Bonifacio High Street was part of Fort McKinley, a 25.78 square kilometer United States Army camp established south of the Pasig River during the American colonial period and named after President William McKinley. Fort McKinley served as the headquarters of the Philippine Scouts, the Philippine Division of the US Army. The facility was turned over to the Philippine government in 1949 and renamed Fort Bonifacio in 1957, after which it became the permanent headquarters of the Philippine Army.

In 1995, the Bases Conversion and Development Authority (BCDA) entered into a joint venture with a private consortium led by Ayala Land and Evergreen Holdings of the Campos Group to redevelop a 240 hectare section of the former military base. The vehicle for this was the Fort Bonifacio Development Corporation (FBDC). Ayala Corporation, through Ayala Land, and Evergreen Holdings later bought a controlling stake in 2003 from Metro Pacific, and together with BCDA they continue to oversee the master plan of what is now Bonifacio Global City.

Bonifacio High Street itself was developed by Ayala Land as the retail spine of BGC and opened in 2007 along 5th Avenue. It was the first project in the Philippines to use the open-air main street format instead of an enclosed mall, taking cues from European downtown shopping streets. The original two blocks were extended over the years. High Street Central opened in May 2012, the indoor Central Square run by SSI Group opened in June 2014, and One Bonifacio High Street, beside the unified Philippine Stock Exchange building, opened in August 2018.
history.citation_label: Wikipedia, Bonifacio Global City official site

place_intro:
Festival Mall sits inside Filinvest City along Corporate Avenue in Alabang, Muntinlupa, at the south end of Metro Manila. It is the main mall of the 244-hectare Filinvest City CBD, surrounded by office towers, condos, the Westgate strip, and Northgate Cyberzone. People from Las Pinas, Paranaque, Bacoor, and Dasmarinas treat it as their default mall because it sits right off the Filinvest exit of SLEX.

The complex is split into wings and outdoor zones locals navigate by name. The Main Wing holds the original anchors including Robinsons Department Store, Landmark Alabang, Shopwise, and Ace Hardware. The East Wing draws families for newer retail and dining. River Park is the open-air strip along the artificial waterway, popular for weekend walks and event setups. The Water Garden is the koi-pond plaza often used for photos.

The crowd skews family and south-Metro residents rather than tourists. You see strollers in the morning, students and Asian Hospital staff at lunch, then BPO workers from Filinvest City offices in the late afternoon. Parking is large and surface-level on several sides, part of why drivers from the south prefer it over tighter malls deeper in the city.

history.heading: How Festival Mall came to be
history.body:
Festival Mall opened on May 15, 1998, built and operated by Filinvest Land, Inc., the property arm of Filinvest Development Corporation. It was launched as Festival Supermall and was positioned from the start as the retail anchor of Filinvest City, the master-planned central business district rising on the Alabang side of Muntinlupa.

The land it stands on has a much older story. The 244-hectare Filinvest City property was formerly the Alabang Stock Farm, a government livestock and biological research facility. A 1924 Art Deco building that belonged to the Department of Health's Biological Production Service was preserved on the site and later restored in 2009, then folded into the mall complex. Filinvest Alabang, Inc. was incorporated on August 25, 1993 as a joint venture to develop the stock farm into a mixed-use district, with horizontal works starting around 1995. Festival Mall was one of the first big pieces of that plan to open to the public.

The mall grew in phases after its 1998 launch. River Park, the four-level expansion along the waterway, was completed in 2014. The East Wing expansion followed, and The Landmark Alabang department store opened inside the mall on July 28, 2017. Around the same time, Filinvest City's overall master plan was updated in 2017 with input from AECOM, which reshaped the public spaces around the mall including Spectrum Linear Park. The Our Lady of Lourdes Chapel inside the mall was designed by Tokyo-based architect Hiroshi Nakamura together with NAP Architects. Today the mall reports a gross floor area of about 400,000 square meters with roughly 170,000 square meters of leasable space, making it one of the larger malls in the south.

place_intro:
Gateway Mall sits at the center of Araneta City in Cubao, Quezon City, the 35-hectare estate bounded by EDSA, Aurora Boulevard, P. Tuazon, and 15th Avenue. It is one of the easiest spots in Metro Manila to reach by transit. The LRT-2 Araneta-Cubao station connects to the mall by a covered walkway, and the MRT-3 station sits across EDSA. Buses, jeepneys, and UV Express vans stop within walking distance.

The property is two malls now. Gateway 1 faces General Aguinaldo Avenue and houses Rustan's Department Store, Rustan's Fresh Supermarket, an Ayala Malls Cinemas branch, and around 200 tenants across five retail levels above three basement parking floors. Gateway Mall 2, which opened in 2023 on the western side of the Smart Araneta Coliseum, is an eight-level building with a luxury and entertainment lean, an Ibis Styles hotel on the upper floors, and a Shopwise grocery on its south wing.

The crowd mixes office workers from Aurora Tower and Manhattan Garden City, students from nearby campuses, concert-goers heading into the Coliseum, and families from eastern Quezon City. Footbridges link Gateway 1, Gateway 2, Farmers Plaza, Ali Mall, and the Coliseum.

history.heading: How Gateway Mall came to be
history.body:
The land under Gateway Mall has a long backstory. In 1952, J. Amado Araneta bought 35 hectares of Cubao property from the Radio Corporation of America, which had used the high ground after the war as a signal site with 18 transmission towers. Araneta wanted to build a multi-purpose arena inspired by Madison Square Garden, and from 1957 to 1959 the Smart Araneta Coliseum was constructed by architect Dominador Lacson Lugtu and engineer Leonardo Onjunco Lugtu. The Coliseum opened in 1960 and triggered a wave of development through the following decades, including Ali Mall, Farmers Plaza, Farmers Market, COD Department Store, and Rustan's Superstore.

By the late 1990s the Araneta Center had aged, and the family began a long-term redevelopment plan estimated at over 30 billion pesos. The first phase started in 2002 with the construction of Gateway Mall. Araneta Center Inc. tapped Felino Palafox of Palafox Associates and Sudhakar Thakurdesai of RTKL Associates to plan and design the project. Gateway Mall opened in 2004 with around 100,000 square meters of leasable space across five retail floors and three parking basements, anchored by Rustan's Department Store. The Philippine Retailers Association named it Shopping Center of the Year in 2006 and 2007.

The expansion known as Gateway Mall 2 broke ground in 2017 on the western side of the Coliseum, with the mall itself and an attached Ibis Styles hotel built together. Shopwise opened its Gateway Mall 2 branch on July 28, 2023, and the mall formally opened in September 2023. The Araneta Center was rebranded as Araneta City in 2019, and the wider district now includes the Manhattan Garden City residential towers developed jointly with Megaworld Corporation.

place_intro:
Robinsons Galleria sits on the corner of EDSA and Ortigas Avenue, on the Quezon City side of Ortigas Center. It is one of the most recognizable landmarks along that stretch of EDSA, across from the EDSA Shrine and within walking distance of Ortigas MRT-3 station. The address falls in Barangay Ugong Norte, Quezon City, though people often lump it with the wider Ortigas district that spills into Pasig and Mandaluyong.

The complex is more than a mall. It includes two office towers, Galleria Corporate Center and Robinsons Equitable Tower, two hotels, Holiday Inn and Suites Manila Galleria and Crowne Plaza Manila Galleria, and the Galleria Regency serviced residences. The five level mall is anchored by Robinsons Department Store and Robinsons Supermarket, with a cinema complex, a chapel, and an ice skating rink from the early years.

Popular sub-areas include the Veranda wing for casual dining, the Pegasus Tower section near the supermarket, and the cinema lobby upstairs. The crowd is a mix of corporate lunch goers on weekdays and family shoppers on weekends. Compared to the denser SM Megamall nearby, Galleria feels calmer and easier to walk through.

history.heading: How Robinsons Galleria began
history.body:
Robinsons Galleria opened on January 12, 1990, making it the first shopping mall built by the Gokongwei family's retail arm, Robinsons Land Corporation. Before the mall stood there, the lot was open land owned by the Social Security System inside what was then a still developing Ortigas Central Business District. John Gokongwei bought a large portion of the property from the SSS in 1987, and construction began in mid 1988 and finished in late 1989.

The site carried symbolic weight even before the mall existed. During the 1986 People Power Revolution, the same stretch along EDSA and Ortigas was one of the points where military tanks were halted by civilians. When Galleria opened a few years later, it sat directly across from the newly built EDSA Shrine, which was expected to give the new development steady foot traffic and visibility.

The mall went through a competitive period in the 1990s after SM Megamall opened in 1991 and Shangri La Plaza followed shortly after, both within the same Ortigas commercial cluster. Galleria held its ground through repeat customers and its mixed use complex of offices, hotels, and serviced apartments. A larger redevelopment program started around 2012 and accelerated from 2016 onward, expanding the retail floor area to roughly 216,000 square meters and refreshing the interiors with new tenants, updated facades, and reconfigured dining wings. The property is still operated by Robinsons Land Corporation under the JG Summit group.

place_intro:
Uptown Mall sits along 36th Street in Uptown Bonifacio, the northern end of Bonifacio Global City in Taguig. It is the centerpiece of the Uptown Bonifacio township by Megaworld, set across from the institutional zone of BGC and within walking distance of 9th Avenue, 38th Street, and the residential cluster around Uptown Parksuites and One Uptown Residence. The mall connects directly to four office towers and several condominiums.

The mall covers about 8.5 hectares of gross floor area, with five levels above ground and six basement parking floors. The layout splits between a high street wing facing 36th Street and an open-air section called The Deck on the fourth level, where the St. Gabriel the Archangel Chapel sits beside al fresco restaurants. A choreographed fountain runs evening shows at the main entrance.

People come for the international flagships, the cinema, the chapel mass schedules, and the Uptown Parade dining row across the street. The crowd skews younger and more office-driven than at Bonifacio High Street. Drivers enter from 11th Avenue or 38th Street, while commuters walk in from the BGC bus stops on 9th Avenue.

history.heading: How Uptown Mall came to be
history.body:
The land that Uptown Mall sits on is part of the former Fort Bonifacio military reservation, a 440 hectare base that the Philippine government opened for commercial conversion through the Bases Conversion and Development Authority during the administration of Fidel Ramos. On February 3, 1995, the BCDA and a consortium led by Metro Pacific Investments Corporation formed the Fort Bonifacio Development Corporation, with the private group taking a 55 percent stake and BCDA holding the remaining 45 percent. The American firm Hellmuth, Obata, and Kassabaum, known as HOK, was appointed in 1996 to draft the master plan that turned the base into what is now Bonifacio Global City.

The northern slice of BGC, which became Uptown Bonifacio, was later developed by Megaworld Corporation, the property arm of Andrew Tan. Megaworld positioned Uptown Bonifacio as a township covering roughly 15.4 hectares, designed around its Live Work Play Learn Shop format and styled after Manhattan in its marketing. The developer committed a multi year capital outlay to build out the residential towers, office buildings, and the central mall on the site.

Uptown Mall opened in November 2015 as the retail anchor of the township. H1 Architecture and Design served as architect for the mall and for Uptown Place Towers 3 and 4, which share the podium. The mall later expanded its draw with the open-air Uptown Parade dining strip across 36th Street, and in the years after opening, Uptown Bonifacio was recognized at the 2018 Philippine Property Awards for its township architectural design. The wider district continues to fill in as Megaworld delivers further residential and office phases around the mall.

place_intro:
Binondo sits on the north bank of the Pasig River in the City of Manila, across from Intramuros and reached via Jones Bridge or the Binondo-Intramuros Bridge. It is widely cited as the oldest Chinatown in the world, and still functions as Manila's main hub for Chinese-Filipino commerce, traditional medicine shops, gold dealers, and old wholesale trading houses. The red Filipino-Chinese Friendship Arch on Quintin Paredes Road is the common entry point.

The district is small enough to cover on foot. Ongpin Street is the spine that most visitors walk first, lined with old tea houses, hopia bakeries, and lechon shops. Quintin Paredes Road carries the bank branches and remittance centers. Escolta, near the river, was once called the Wall Street of the Philippines and still holds prewar buildings like the Calvo, Regina, and Natividad. Carvajal is the wet market alley off Nueva.

Visitors are a mix of Manilenos doing weekend errands, Chinese-Filipino families going to Binondo Church for Sunday Mass, heritage walkers tracing Escolta facades, and DIY tourists doing the crawl from Ongpin to Plaza San Lorenzo Ruiz. The feel is working district first, tourist district second.

history.heading: How Binondo became the world's oldest Chinatown
history.body:
Binondo was established in 1594 by Spanish Governor-General Luis Perez Dasmarinas as a permanent settlement for Catholic Chinese, then called sangleys. The Spanish authorities granted the land across the Pasig River from Intramuros to a group of Chinese merchants and artisans in perpetuity, tax-free and with limited self-governing privileges. The site was chosen so the colonial administration in the walled city could watch the migrant community from a safe distance, especially after the 1593 mutiny that killed Dasmarinas's father Gomez aboard a galley manned by sangley rowers.

The Dominican Order took charge of the parish in 1596 and founded the church now known as Binondo Church, originally dedicated to Saint Gabriel the Archangel. The Dominicans converted many residents to Catholicism, and intermarriage with indigenous Filipino women gave rise to the Chinese mestizo community. The original church was destroyed by British bombardment during the 1762 occupation of Manila, rebuilt, then damaged again in the 1863 earthquake. The granite church standing today was completed in 1852 and lost much of its structure during World War II.

By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Binondo had grown into the financial center of the Philippines, with Escolta housing the offices of British and American banks. After World War II, much of the formal banking moved to Makati, but wholesale trade, hardware, textile, and traditional Chinese retail stayed. Pope John Paul II raised Binondo Church to Minor Basilica in 1992, and in September 2024 the Catholic Bishops Conference of the Philippines elevated it to a national shrine dedicated to San Lorenzo Ruiz, the first Filipino saint. In 2024, Binondo marked its 430th year.

place_intro:
Cubao is a commercial district in Quezon City at the intersection of EDSA and Aurora Boulevard, in Barangay Socorro. Most people use "Cubao" interchangeably with Araneta City, the 35-hectare mixed-use complex that anchors the area. It is one of Metro Manila's busiest transit zones, with the LRT 2 Araneta Center-Cubao station, the MRT 3 Cubao station, and the Araneta Bus Terminal all within walking distance.

Sub-areas inside Araneta City include Gateway Mall and Gateway Mall 2 on the north, the Smart Araneta Coliseum in the middle, Ali Mall to the south, and Farmers Market on the eastern edge near the MRT. Farmers Market is the public market that older Manilenos still travel across the city for. Outside the Araneta perimeter, wider Cubao covers the Cubao Expo strip on General Romulo Avenue and residential blocks around 15th Avenue and P. Tuazon.

The crowd is mixed: commuters catching a provincial bus, concertgoers heading to the Big Dome, students, families on weekend market runs, and regulars who have shopped here since the 1970s. Cubao is not polished the way BGC is, and that is part of the appeal. You can cover a coliseum show, a grocery run, and a meal without moving your car.

history.heading: How Cubao became Araneta City
history.body:
The land that makes up today's Araneta City was bought by businessman J. Amado Araneta in 1952 from the Radio Corporation of America (RCA). Before the purchase, the 35-hectare lot held RCA's broadcast complex and was dotted with radio towers, sitting on what was then the edge of southern Quezon City. The Araneta family had lost their original mansion on Taft Avenue during the Battle of Manila in 1945, and the Cubao parcel became the base for a new ambition: a self-contained commercial and entertainment district built around a central landmark.

That central landmark was the Araneta Coliseum, now known as the Smart Araneta Coliseum or the Big Dome. Construction started in 1957 and the venue opened on March 16, 1960 with a world junior lightweight title fight where Gabriel "Flash" Elorde defended his crown against Johnny Saxton. At the time of its opening it was the largest covered coliseum in the world, and the master plan radiated outward from it. Over the 1960s and 1970s the area filled in with the country's first strip mall along Aurora Boulevard, Fiesta Carnival, Ali Mall (which opened in 1976 and was named after Muhammad Ali following the Thrilla in Manila), Farmers Market, and the New Frontier Theater.

Development slowed in the 1980s and 1990s, then picked up again once LRT Line 2 was planned through the district. Jorge L. Araneta, son of J. Amado, launched a multi-phase redevelopment plan in the early 2000s. Gateway Mall opened in 2004 as the first major piece. The complex was rebranded from Araneta Center to Araneta City in 2019, and Gateway Mall 2 opened in 2023 on the western side of the coliseum.

place_intro:
Tomas Morato Avenue is a long restaurant row in Quezon City that runs through the South Triangle area, between EDSA and the Scout neighborhood. The avenue meets Timog Avenue at the 11th World Scout Jamboree Memorial Rotonda, the usual meeting point when planning a night out. Most people just say "Morato" when telling a driver where to go.

The strip is officially a Tourism District of Quezon City, often called the Tomas Morato and Timog lifestyle district. The corridor is packed end to end with sit-down restaurants, chain cafes, bakeshops, karaoke bars, comedy clubs, and concept places tucked inside old residential houses. Side streets named after the Scouts, like Sct. Albano, Sct. Lazcano, Sct. Tobias, and Sct. Borromeo, branch off and hold even more places to eat. Limbaga Street is one of those Scout streets and has its own pocket of restaurants.

The crowd is a mix of Quezon City residents, ABS-CBN and GMA Network staff from the nearby broadcast complexes, call center workers from offices along Timog and Mother Ignacia, students, and families on weekend dinners. Compared to BGC or Makati, the vibe is older and less corporate, with many restaurants running since the 1980s.

history.heading: How Tomas Morato Avenue got its name
history.body:
The avenue traces back to a 1938 government project under President Manuel L. Quezon, who used the People's Homesite Corporation, the precursor of the National Housing Authority, to plan an affordable housing district for working-class families being pushed out of Manila. When Quezon City was officially created on October 12, 1939, Quezon convinced his longtime friend Tomas Eduardo Bernabeu Morato, a Spanish-born Filipino businessman from Alicante, to serve as the new city's first mayor. Morato took office on November 9, 1939 and served until 1942.

The road opened around 1940, during Morato's term as mayor, and was originally called Sampaloc Avenue because of the tamarind trees lining it. Local accounts say Morato, Quezon, and journalist Alejandro Roces planted some of those trees themselves. In 1965, Congress passed a law renaming Sampaloc Avenue to Tomas Morato Avenue in honor of the late first mayor, who died that same year. The change took effect in 1966.

The surrounding street grid took on its current identity after July 28, 1963, when a plane carrying the Philippine delegation to the 11th World Scout Jamboree crashed into the Arabian Sea near Bombay, India. Twenty four Filipinos died, including 22 Boy Scouts. Quezon City renamed the streets around Tomas Morato after the fallen delegates, so you now see Sct. Albano, Sct. Lazcano, Sct. Fuentebella, and Sct. Borromeo. The 11th World Scout Jamboree Memorial Rotonda, with 24 statues representing the delegates, was later built at the intersection of Tomas Morato and Timog, and the barangay around it was named Laging Handa. The restaurant strip grew out of this Scout area through the 1980s and 1990s.

place_intro:
Maginhawa Street runs through Teachers Village in Diliman, Quezon City, a short ride from the University of the Philippines campus. The strip sits inside a planned residential grid where streets are named after Filipino virtues like Malumanay and Masikap. Maginhawa itself roughly means "at ease." The road stretches a little over two kilometers and crosses five barangays: UP Village, Teachers Village West, Teachers Village East, Malaya, and Sikatuna.

The character changes block by block. The section near V. Luna Extension feels residential, with older two-story houses turned into small cafes. The middle stretch, between Malingap and Mayumi, is where most of the foot traffic concentrates, with restaurants packed into former garages and living rooms. The far end near Kalayaan Avenue mixes student housing, churches, and quiet side streets that loop back into the village grid.

Most people who come here are UP and Ateneo students, young professionals from nearby BPO offices, and QC locals doing a weekend crawl. The crowd skews young and walks the strip since parking is tight. Magiting, Malingap, and Mayaman branch off Maginhawa and are treated as part of the same hub.

history.heading: How Maginhawa became Maginhawa
history.body:
Teachers Village was laid out in the 1950s as a planned residential subdivision in Diliman, Quezon City, on land adjacent to the University of the Philippines campus. The People's Homesite and Housing Corporation, the government housing body that built much of postwar Quezon City, opened the area for residential use and later distributed parcels to public school teachers, which gave the village its name. The street grid was named after Tagalog adjectives describing positive traits, and Maginhawa was one of the longer through-roads cutting across the subdivision.

For decades the street stayed mostly residential, with a few longstanding establishments serving UP students and faculty. Rodic's Diner, known for its tapsilog, opened in the area in the 1980s and is often cited as the early anchor that drew students off campus and onto the street. Schools like Holy Family School of Quezon City, opened in 1966 by the Siervas de San Jose, and Claret School of Quezon City, opened in 1967 by the Claretian Missionaries at the corner of Maginhawa and Mayumi, added steady foot traffic to the area.

The shift into a food strip picked up around 2010, when small owner-run restaurants started taking over converted homes along Maginhawa. The Quezon City government organized the first Quezon City Food Festival on the street in 2014, which pushed the area into mainstream attention. In 2015, the Quezon City Council passed Ordinance No. 2439 declaring Maginhawa, V. Luna Extension, Malingap, and Magiting streets as the Maginhawa Art and Food Hub, the city's first official food tourism district. Ordinance No. 2559 followed in 2017, adding Mayaman Street and extending the hub to about 2.2 kilometers across five barangays.

place_intro:
Burgos Circle, also called Padre Burgos Circle, is a small traffic roundabout and park inside Forbestown Center, a pocket of Bonifacio Global City in Taguig. It sits where Forbestown Road, 1st Avenue, 2nd Avenue, and 29th Street meet, a short walk south of Bonifacio High Street and a few blocks from Market! Market! and Serendra. Locals usually refer to the whole low-rise strip of restaurants around the park simply as "Burgos Circle."

The character here is different from the rest of BGC. Instead of glass office towers, you get a ring of two-storey commercial blocks with al fresco seating, condo lobbies of Bellagio, Forbeswood Heights, and Forbeswood Parklane facing inward, and a circular park at the center with a sculpture called "The Trees" by Reynato Paz Contreras. Benches, jogging paths, and a small lawn fill the park where dog walkers and families gather on weekends.

Young professionals from nearby offices, expats from Forbes Town condos, families from Serendra, and out-of-town visitors come here for a slower, walkable side of Taguig. Most arrive on foot or via the BGC Bus stop on 28th Street, then loop around the park before settling into one of the perimeter restaurants.

history.heading: How Burgos Circle came to be
history.body:
Burgos Circle is part of Forbes Town Center, a roughly five-hectare mixed-use township that Megaworld Corporation developed inside Bonifacio Global City through a joint venture with Bonifacio West Development Corporation. The land was part of the former Fort Bonifacio military reservation, which the Bases Conversion and Development Authority (BCDA) opened up for private development in 1995 through the Fort Bonifacio Development Corporation, a joint venture with private partners including Ayala Land and Megaworld.

Forbes Town Center was conceptualized as Megaworld's first township inside BGC, and was inspired by Bugis Village in Singapore, with a low-rise retail and dining ring wrapped around residential towers. The first residential phase, Forbeswood Heights, was launched in June 2003, followed by The Bellagio and Forbeswood Parklane in the years after. The roundabout at the center of the township was named after Jose Burgos, one of the three martyred priests collectively known as Gomburza.

The circle became widely known around 2009, when it was identified in local press as a new dining hotspot, often compared in coverage to Greenbelt 2, Serendra, and the old Remedios Circle in Malate. Underneath the park sits a 72-meter wide, 12-meter deep retarding basin built as part of BGC's flood control system, with a capacity of about 22,000 cubic meters that drains into creeks leading to Manila Bay. At the center of the park is The Trees, a sculpture by Reynato Paz Contreras, recorded as the first piece of public art installed in Bonifacio Global City.

place_intro:
Tagaytay City sits in the northern uplands of Cavite, perched on the Tagaytay Ridge that runs about 32 kilometers from Mount Batulao to Mount Sungay. The ridge averages around 600 meters above sea level, so the air stays cooler than the lowlands. Almost the entire city looks down on Taal Lake and the small island volcano in its middle, which is why people drive up from Manila on weekends. The city covers around 65 square kilometers across 34 barangays.

Tagaytay has no single downtown. Commercial life clusters along Aguinaldo Highway and the Tagaytay-Nasugbu Road. Mendez Crossing, Silang Crossing East, the City Market at Tolentino East, and the Mahogany Beef Market at Kaybagal South all serve as hubs. Ayala Malls Serin, Robinsons Summit Ridge, and SM City Tagaytay anchor the retail strips, while Sky Ranch beside Taal Vista Hotel pulls in families.

Day-trippers, couples on staycations, retreat groups, and Manila weekenders make up most of the traffic, with crowds thickening Friday to Sunday. Common stops include People's Park in the Sky on Mount Sungay, Picnic Grove on the ridge edge, and the view restaurants facing the lake. Hotels cluster around Kaybagal and Maharlika East.

history.heading: How Tagaytay became a city on the ridge
history.body:
Long before it was a city, the Tagaytay Ridge was already known to people from the surrounding Cavite and Batangas towns. The name itself comes from the Tagalog word tagaytay or taytay, meaning a mountain ridge or low mountain range, a direct reference to the spine of land the city now sits on. During the Philippine Revolution against Spain in the late 1890s, the ridge served as a refuge and movement corridor for revolutionaries from Batangas, Laguna, and nearby Cavite towns, since the high ground gave cover and a clear line of sight over the lake basin.

Tagaytay was formally created as a chartered city through Commonwealth Act No. 338, signed by President Manuel L. Quezon on June 21, 1938. The bill was authored by Representative Justiniano Montano of Cavite. To form the new city, land was carved out from the existing towns of Silang, Mendez, Indang, and Amadeo, all in Cavite. The charter set out the city's boundaries, governance structure, and administrative officers, and is on record with the Official Gazette and the Supreme Court E-Library.

Through the post-war decades, Tagaytay grew slowly as a quiet upland town known for its cool weather, which is how it picked up the informal tag of a second summer capital after Baguio. Government rest houses, retreat centers run by religious orders, and a handful of view hotels including Taal Vista, which dates back to the 1930s, set the early tourism pattern. Heavier development came later, with Sky Ranch opening beside Taal Vista in 2013 and Ayala Malls Serin opening along Aguinaldo Highway in 2015, shifting Tagaytay from a sleepy lookout town into the weekend destination it is now. The 2020 Taal Volcano eruption briefly disrupted tourism, but the ridge recovered as one of the most visited spots in Calabarzon.

place_intro:
Baguio City sits at around 1,500 meters above sea level in the Cordillera mountain range, about five to six hours north of Manila by bus through Tarlac and La Union. It is a highly urbanized city inside Benguet and the regional center of the Cordillera Administrative Region. The cool climate is the main draw, which is why it carries the title Summer Capital of the Philippines.

The central business district runs along Session Road, Magsaysay, Abanao, and Harrison, with Burnham Park anchoring the middle of the city and SM Baguio on the high ground above it. Visitors circulate between this core and the surrounding hills, including Camp John Hay on the southeast, Wright Park and The Mansion along Leonard Wood Road, and Mines View Park on the northeastern edge.

The crowd mixes local Igorot residents, students from UP Baguio and Saint Louis University, weekend road trippers from Metro Manila, and long-stay travelers. Traffic builds heavily during Holy Week, the Panagbenga flower festival in February, and Christmas, so DIY visitors usually base near Session Road or Upper General Luna.

history.heading: How Baguio became the Summer Capital
history.body:
Before the Americans arrived, the area was an Ibaloi settlement called Kafagway, a small rancheria of around twenty houses with land largely held by the Ibaloi leader Mateo Carino. The name Baguio comes from the Ibaloi word bag-iw, meaning moss, which the Spaniards later spelled as Baguio. The Spanish presence in the highlands was light, and the town remained a quiet pastoral area until the American colonial period.

In 1900, the United States established Baguio as a hill station, the only American hill station in Asia, drawn by the cool climate at 1,500 meters elevation. In October 1903, President Theodore Roosevelt signed an executive order setting aside land in Benguet for a military rest reservation, which became Camp John Hay, named after his Secretary of State John Milton Hay. The Philippine Commission held sessions in Baguio from April to June 1904 at the Baden-Powell Hall, and the route leading to that hall is the origin of the name Session Road.

The master plan was drawn by American architect and urban planner Daniel Hudson Burnham, who visited Baguio in December 1904 and submitted his final designs to the US Congress in June 1905. Burnham planned the city for 25,000 to 30,000 people, following the City Beautiful movement with broad avenues and a civic core radiating from open green space, the layout now preserved at Burnham Park. The Mansion, the official summer residence of the President of the Philippines, was designed by architect William E. Parsons based on Burnham's preliminary plans. On September 1, 1909, Baguio was formally declared a chartered city through Act No. 1963 of the Philippine Commission, and the designation Summer Capital of the Philippines was applied at that time.

place_intro:
Cebu City sits on the eastern coast of Cebu island in Central Visayas, facing Mactan across a narrow channel. It is the capital of Cebu province but governs itself as a highly urbanized city, independent from the province. The city runs from the port through the dense downtown grid and uphill into the cooler hills of the north. Locals call it the Queen City of the South.

The city splits into two legislative districts. The North District covers the commercial belt, including Lahug, Banilad, Mabolo, and the Cebu IT Park area, where call centers, condos, and late-night spots cluster around a walkable loop. The South District covers Carbon, Pasil, and the older port-adjacent areas near Fort San Pedro and Plaza Independencia. Capitol Site and Fuente Osmena Circle sit in the middle, ringed by hospitals, hotels, and the Cebu Provincial Capitol at the top of Osmena Boulevard.

DIY travelers usually base near IT Park or Fuente for easy taxi access, or in Lahug and Banilad for closer reach to Ayala Center Cebu and SM Seaside. Backpackers stay near Colon Street and the Basilica del Santo Nino. Traffic is heavy, so most plan trips by area.

history.heading: How Cebu City became the Queen City of the South
history.body:
Cebu was already an established trading port long before any European arrived. The harbors of Sugbo, the precolonial name of the settlement, were known as sinibuayng hingpit, meaning the place for trading, and the modern name Cebu traces back to this term. Under Rajah Humabon, Sugbo was part of regional Asian trade networks dealing in goods from China, Borneo, and surrounding islands.

Ferdinand Magellan landed on Cebu in 1521 and formed an alliance with Rajah Humabon, who was baptized into Christianity along with his queen. Magellan was killed shortly after at the Battle of Mactan by the forces of Lapu-Lapu. The Spanish returned in force on April 15, 1565, under Miguel Lopez de Legazpi. The Treaty of Cebu was formalized on July 3, 1565, and Legazpi named the settlement Villa de San Miguel de Cebu, later renamed Ciudad del Santisimo Nombre de Jesus.
