The Ortigas Center buildout on the Pasig side, led by Ortigas and Company Limited Partnership on land of the former Hacienda de Mandaloyon, is what turned the western edge of the city from farmland and low-density housing into the second-largest central business district in Metro Manila after Makati.

place_intro:
SM City Clark sits along Manuel A. Roxas Highway in Barangay Malabanias, inside the Clark Freeport Zone in Angeles City, Pampanga. It is the first big landmark most travelers see after rolling out of Clark International Airport, since the mall is just beside the Clark Main Gate and a short ride from the SM transport terminal where buses to Manila, Subic, Baguio, and Tarlac queue all day.

The footprint covers around 308,909 square meters of gross floor area after the 2022 expansion, with more than 700 shops, the SM Store and SM Hypermarket as anchors, an IMAX cinema, a bowling center, the SMX Convention Center Clark, a Park Inn by Radisson Blu hotel, and a National University campus on the same complex. The layout is split between the original main mall and a newer wing connected by covered walkways.

The crowd is a mix of Pampanga and Tarlac families on weekends, Korean residents from the nearby Clark and Friendship areas, expats working in the Freeport BPO towers, and travelers killing time before early flights at CRK. It feels more spread out than SM City Pampanga in San Fernando, so road-trippers from Manila treat it as a stopover.

history.heading: From American base land to Luzon's biggest SM outside Metro Manila
history.body:
SM City Clark opened on May 12, 2006 as the 24th supermall of SM Prime Holdings, the listed mall arm of the Sy family group. It was the second SM mall in Pampanga after SM City Pampanga in San Fernando and Mexico, and it was the first major retail anchor built inside the Clark Freeport Zone, the former Clark Air Base that the United States Air Force handed over to the Philippine government in 1991 and that was later converted into a Special Economic Zone managed by the Clark Development Corporation.

The mall was planted on land leased from CDC along M.A. Roxas Highway in Barangay Malabanias, very close to the Main Gate. The site was part of the rolling frontage of the old base, and the choice of location was deliberate. SM Prime wanted a Central Luzon flagship near the new Clark International Airport and the expressway network linking NLEX and SCTEX, so domestic and Korean tourists would have a familiar SM stop the moment they cleared the gate.

The complex was expanded in stages. A second phase added a larger food court, the Meeting Place activity area, and BPO office space in 2013. In 2022, SM Prime opened the SMX Convention Center Clark on the property together with four additional BPO buildings and the new skyline wing, which pushed the gross floor area to 308,909 square meters and made SM City Clark the largest shopping mall in Luzon outside Metro Manila and the eighth largest in the country.

place_intro:
Panglao Island sits in the Bohol Sea just southwest of mainland Bohol, linked to Tagbilaran by two short causeway bridges. The island covers around 91 square kilometers of mostly level coral limestone, split between two municipalities, Panglao on the southwest side and Dauis closer to the bridges. Panglao town holds ten barangays plus three offshore islands at Balicasag, Gak-ang, and Pontod.

The tourist core for most DIY travelers is barangay Tawala, where Alona Beach runs about a kilometer of white sand lined with resorts, dive shops, restaurants, and bars. Alona is the loud, social strip with the heaviest foreign and Manila crowd, while Bolod, Danao, Doljo, and Libaong on the same southern coast feel quieter, with longer stretches of empty sand and small family-run stays. The Poblacion keeps the town hall, public market, and old parish church.

The Bohol-Panglao International Airport opened on November 28, 2018, about two kilometers from Alona, so most visitors land, grab a tricycle or van south, and base in Tawala or Bolod. From there, day trips to Balicasag for snorkeling, dolphin watching off Pamilacan, and the Chocolate Hills and Loboc River are standard routes.

history.heading: From Spanish parish to international beach hub
history.body:
Panglao Island shows up in the Bohol record long before tourism. Archaeological finds in Dauis and Panglao include trade ceramics from the Chinese Ming era, which place the area inside the pre-colonial Visayan trade network well before the Spaniards arrived in the wider Philippines in 1521. The locals were already coastal traders and fishers, and the island name itself is commonly traced either to Panglawod, meaning toward the open sea, or to panggaw, a traditional fishing implement.

Under Spanish rule, Jesuits set up a mission post on the island and in 1782 formed it into a parish under the name La Iglesia de San Agustin de Panglawod. Panglao was then formally organized as a municipality in 1803, which is the date most local sources cite as the town's official founding. The Panglao watchtower, built in 1851 along the coast as part of the Spanish coastal defense system against Moro raids, still stands today and is recognized as the tallest of its kind among Philippine octagonal watchtowers.

The modern beach-tourism identity is much newer. Alona Beach in barangay Tawala took its name from Filipino actress Alona Alegre after a 1973 scene filmed there for the movie Esteban with Fernando Poe Jr., and the strip slowly grew through the 1990s and 2000s from a backpacker and dive spot into the resort row it is now. The opening of Bohol-Panglao International Airport on November 28, 2018, replacing the old Tagbilaran Airport on the mainland, pushed Panglao into its current role as the main air gateway for Bohol and the busiest beach destination in the province.

place_intro:
Cabanatuan City sits in the south of Nueva Ecija, in Central Luzon, about two hours from Manila by bus along the Pan-Philippine Highway. It is a component city, not the provincial capital (that belongs to Palayan since 1965), but it is the economic heart of the province and the most populous city in Nueva Ecija, with about 343,672 residents recorded in the 2024 census, spread across 89 barangays.

The downtown is compact and walkable. Burgos Avenue and Del Pilar Street carry most of the traffic through the city proper, while General Tinio and Rizal Streets cut east to west, and Mabini Street is the university belt. Maharlika Highway runs the length of the city from Barangay Sumacab Este up to Caalibangbangan, with most newer commercial growth, including SM City Cabanatuan, clustered along that corridor.

The crowd is a mix of farmers and traders from surrounding Nueva Ecija towns, students from the three universities, and travelers passing through to Cagayan Valley. Cabanatuan is widely called the Tricycle Capital of the Philippines, with more than 30,000 motorized tricycles working the streets.

history.heading: From a Gapan barrio in 1750 to a chartered city in 1950
history.body:
Cabanatuan started as a barrio of Gapan in 1750. By 1780 it had grown enough to be separated as its own municipality and was named the capital of La Provincia de Nueva Ecija under Spanish administration. The early settlers were mostly Tagalogs who came in from Bulacan and from Morong, which is now part of Rizal Province, with later arrivals from Ilocos, Pampanga, and Tayabas (present-day Quezon).

The town lost its capital status in 1850 when the seat of Nueva Ecija was transferred to San Isidro. It only regained the title in 1917, after the American-era Administrative Code took effect. That second run as capital ended in 1965, when Congress created Palayan City and made it the permanent provincial capital.

The name itself has no single confirmed origin. Local accounts point to three theories: a corruption of Cabatuan, referring to the rocky river country in the area; the phrase Kaban ng Tuwa, a nod to the rice harvests of the plain; and most commonly Banatu, a sturdy vine that once grew along the banks of the Rio Grande de Pampanga.

Cabanatuan became a chartered city through Republic Act No. 526, signed on June 16, 1950. The most internationally known chapter of its history is from World War II. The Japanese ran the Cabanatuan Prison Camp on the city's outskirts, holding American and Allied prisoners, including survivors of the Bataan Death March. In January 1945, the U.S. Army 6th Ranger Battalion moved about 30 miles behind Japanese lines and freed the surviving POWs in what is now remembered as the Raid at Cabanatuan.

place_intro:
Marikina City sits on the eastern edge of Metro Manila, about 21 kilometers from the City of Manila, inside the Marikina Valley. It borders Quezon City to the west, Pasig and Cainta to the south, San Mateo, Rizal to the north, and Antipolo to the east. The Marikina River cuts through the mid-western section, running about 11 kilometers and forming the spine of the local park system.

The city is split into two congressional districts and 16 barangays. District I covers Barangka, Tanong, Jesus dela Pena, IVC, Kalumpang, San Roque, Sta. Elena, Sto. Nino, and Malanday. District II covers Concepcion Uno, Concepcion Dos, Nangka, Parang, Marikina Heights, Fortune, and Tumana. Old-town landmarks cluster around Sta. Elena and San Roque near the river, while newer residential pockets sit in Concepcion and Marikina Heights.

Marikina is a mix of shoemaker neighborhoods, riverside parks, and quiet middle-class subdivisions, with the Marikina River Park, Riverbanks Center, and Lilac Street drawing most outside visitors. Crowds skew local and family-oriented, with runners and cyclists on the riverside esplanade in the early morning and food crawlers on Lilac Street by evening.

history.heading: From Jesuit mission town to shoe capital of the Philippines
history.body:
The Augustinians reached the Marikina Valley in 1570 and held the first mass in the area, but the formal founding of the town is traced to the Jesuits in 1630, when the settlement along the river was called Jesus dela Pena, meaning Jesus of the Rocks. The name was later shortened to Mariquina, the form used through the Spanish period and the origin of the present spelling.

Marikina played a brief but notable role during the Philippine Revolution, serving as the provincial capital of the Province of Manila under the First Philippine Republic from 1898 to 1899. The town stayed part of Rizal province for most of the 20th century until Presidential Decree No. 824, signed on November 7, 1975, folded it together with three other Rizal cities, twelve Rizal towns, and Valenzuela in Bulacan into the Metropolitan Manila Area. Cityhood followed later, and Republic Act No. 9364, approved by President Gloria Macapagal Arroyo on December 15, 2006, created the two congressional districts the city uses today.

The shoe industry that the city is now known for began in 1887, when Don Laureano Guevara, known locally as Kapitan Moy, tried to repair a broken pair of imported shoes at his home rather than travel to Manila. Tiburcio Eustaquio, a bakya or wooden clog maker called Busyong, supplied him with shoe lasts so he could study how the parts fit together. Kapitan Moy converted the silong of his house into a small factory and trained fishermen, farmers, and women from the town to make shoes during the off-season for planting and harvest. The original house, now known as the Kapitan Moy Residence in Sta. Elena, still stands as the heritage anchor of the trade, and the Marikina Shoe Museum, opened in 2001, was built to document the same history.

place_intro:
Vigan is the capital of Ilocos Sur on the western coast of Luzon, facing the South China Sea. The city covers 39 barangays, nine forming the poblacion and 30 rural barangays between the Govantes and Mestizo Rivers. Heritage walks happen inside the old Mestizo District, the eastern quarter historically called Kasanglayan, where Calle Crisologo runs as a cobblestone strip lined with bahay na bato houses, shops, and inns.

The townscape keeps its Spanish grid of 25 streets and 233 historic buildings inside roughly 17.25 hectares of heritage core. Plaza Salcedo and Plaza Burgos sit beside the Saint Paul Metropolitan Cathedral, and side streets fan toward the Mestizo River where Chinese junks once docked. Architecture mixes native Filipino, Chinese, and Spanish elements, with capiz windows and brick walls.

Outside the heritage core, Vigan works as a regular capital city with a public market, a university belt around UNP, and rural barangays handling farming, pottery, and weaving. The character is small town and low rise, with kalesas still used as normal transport inside the poblacion. DIY travelers can base inside the heritage zone and reach every major landmark on foot.

history.heading: From Isla de Bigan trading post to UNESCO World Heritage city
history.body:
Long before the Spaniards arrived, Vigan was already a coastal trading post on the Mestizo River. Chinese, Japanese, Arab, Indian, and other Austronesian traders sailed in to barter for gold, beeswax, and mountain goods from the Cordilleras. Chinese merchants called the place Isla de Bigan. One accepted origin story for the name traces it to the Ilocano word bigaa, the tuber plant that grew along the riverbanks.

The Spanish chapter started on June 13, 1572, when Juan de Salcedo arrived in Vigan after marching north from Manila on May 20, 1572. Salcedo founded the settlement as Villa Fernandina de Vigan, named after Prince Ferdinand, son of King Philip II. He patterned the urban plan after Intramuros in Manila, which is why Vigan still has the same checkerboard grid of narrow streets and central plazas you see today. In 1595, the Diocese of Nueva Segovia was established and the first bishop was named, which turned Vigan into the religious capital of the north.

Under Spanish rule the town was split into two zones. The Barrio de Mestizos, also called Kasanglayan or Place of Sangley People, sat on the eastern side along the Mestizo River where Chinese traders and their mestizo descendants were confined. The Barrio de Naturales sat on the western side for the native Ilocanos. This Mestizo District is the same quarter that survives today as the heritage village, including Calle Crisologo.

Vigan was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1999 as the most intact example in Asia of a planned Spanish colonial town. The citation covers the 25 street grid and 233 historic buildings that still stand inside the heritage core.
history.citation_label: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, Historic City of Vigan

place_intro:
Ortigas Center is a central business district that straddles the joint boundaries of Pasig, Mandaluyong, and Quezon City inside Metro Manila. It covers more than 100 hectares of office towers, condos, hotels, and mega-malls, and it ranks as the second largest CBD in the capital region after Makati. EDSA forms the western edge, Ortigas Avenue cuts across the north, Meralco Avenue runs along the east, and Shaw Boulevard sits to the south.

The district is not one neat grid. The western strip near Wack-Wack Greenhills sits inside Mandaluyong, while the office core, the Asian Development Bank compound, and the residential blocks along San Miguel Avenue and Emerald Avenue fall under Pasig. Quezon City clips the northwestern corner near Robinsons Galleria. Locals navigate by anchor buildings: the Galleria side, the Megamall-Podium spine along Doña Julia Vargas Avenue, and the ADB corridor.

By day it runs corporate, with JG Summit Holdings, UnionBank, Meralco, and Robinsons Bank headquartered here. By weekend it tilts toward malls. Footbridges and the MRT-3 Ortigas station knit the area, so most visitors move on foot once inside, even when EDSA traffic at the perimeter stays slow.

history.heading: From Hacienda de Mandaloyon to Metro Manila's second CBD
history.body:
Before it was Ortigas Center, the land was part of the Hacienda de Mandaloyon, a 4,033-hectare estate held by the Augustinian Order that stretched across what are now San Juan, Mandaluyong, Quezon City, and Pasig. On January 20, 1920, the Augustinian friars sold the hacienda to Dr. Frank W. Dudley and Don Francisco Ortigas. Dudley later turned over his share to Phil C. Whitaker, and the venture operated for a time as Whitaker and Ortigas.

On July 10, 1931, the partnership was formally incorporated as Ortigas, Madrigal y cia., S. en C., a limited partnership by shares. The named partners were Francisco Ortigas (Don Paco), Vicente Madrigal, B.C.M. Johnston, Fulgencio Borromeo, Clyde A. Dewitt, and Manuel L. Quezon, who would later become president of the Commonwealth. This is the entity locals today recognize as Ortigas and Company, the master developer that has shaped the district ever since.

For decades the property sat largely undeveloped, used mostly as open land on the edges of postwar Manila. The shift toward a business district began in 1966, when the area was selected to host the headquarters of the Asian Development Bank. Office and commercial build-out followed through the 1970s and 1980s, and in 1982 Ortigas Land helped establish the Ortigas Center Association, Inc. (OCAI) as the non-stock, non-profit body that still manages the district. Anchor malls and towers, including SM Megamall and Robinsons Galleria, opened in the years that followed and locked in the Ortigas-Pasig-Mandaluyong corner as a permanent commercial core.

place_intro:
Bohol is an island province in Central Visayas, made up of the main Bohol island plus 75 smaller surrounding islands, with a land area of about 4,821 square kilometers. The terrain is mostly rolling and hilly, with roughly half the island sitting on limestone and a large interior plateau where the Chocolate Hills rise across Carmen, Sagbayan, and Batuan. UNESCO recognized the whole island as the Bohol Island UNESCO Global Geopark in 2023.

Tagbilaran, the capital, sits on the southwestern coast and is the usual entry point for DIY travelers flying in or arriving by RoRo ferry. The city has 15 barangays, with the urban core in Poblacion I, II, III, and Cogon. The central business district is in Poblacion II and III, while CPG Avenue is the main spine through Cogon and Poblacion. Sitio Ubos, the lower town near the old harbor, is the oldest part of Tagbilaran.

Loboc sits about 24 kilometers inland from Tagbilaran, in a valley split by the Loboc River. The municipality covers around 5,765 hectares across 28 barangays, with the town center beside the river and across the highway from the old Loboc Church. The river starts in Carmen and flows south into the Bohol Sea.

history.heading: From Datu Sikatuna's coast to a Jesuit river town
history.body:
Long before Spanish contact, the settlement that became Tagbilaran was already trading with China and the Malay world. In 1565, Spanish conquistador Miguel Lopez de Legazpi and the local chieftain Datu Sikatuna sealed the famous Sandugo or blood compact along this coast, one of the earliest recorded treaties between Filipinos and Spaniards.

The Jesuits arrived in Bohol on November 17, 1596, when Fr. Gabriel Sanchez and Fr. Juan de Torres landed in Baclayon. Six months later, Fr. Juan de Torres pushed inland up the Loboc River and formally established the town of Loboc on June 29, 1597, under the encomienda of Dona Catalina de Bolanos. Loboc became a parish in 1602, the oldest on the island. After the Moro raid on Baclayon on October 26, 1600, the Jesuits moved their mission center inland to Loboc, which they called the Residencia Boholana, and around 1604 they put up a Seminario-Colegio for native boys. When the Jesuits were expelled in 1768, the parish was handed over to the Augustinian Recollects.

Tagbilaran was carved out as a separate town from Baclayon on February 9, 1742, by General Francisco Antonio Calderon de la Barca, Military Governor of the Visayas. In 1854, it was designated the capital when Bohol was reorganized as a separate politico-military unit. The province in its current form was organized on October 20, 1901, under the Provincial Government Act of the Philippine Commission. Tagbilaran was elevated to a chartered city on July 18, 1966, through Republic Act No. 4660.

place_intro:
Puerto Princesa is the capital of Palawan, sitting on a narrow strip of the island facing the Sulu Sea to the east and the South China Sea to the west. It is bordered on the north by San Vicente and Roxas, and on the south by Aborlan. The city covers around 253,982 hectares, one of the largest by land area in the country, and is split into 66 barangays grouped into two districts. Only 35 are urban, so most of the city is rural land, mountain, and coast.

The downtown core sits along Rizal Avenue and the bayside flats, with Puerto Princesa International Airport right inside the city center, so tricycles from arrivals can drop you in town in 15 to 20 minutes. Mendoza Park, named after Dr. Higinio Mendoza who was executed at Canigaran in 1944, is the first public park and still works as the central plaza, ringed by government offices.

Outside downtown, the city stretches across the Saint Paul Mountain Range in the north and thins to about 8.5 kilometers of land between the two coasts at Bahile. That geography is why one city contains the airport, the Honda Bay piers on the Sulu Sea side, and the road to Sabang on the South China Sea side.

history.heading: From Spanish naval port to Palawan's capital
history.body:
Spanish colonizers formally established Puerto Princesa on March 4, 1872, after surveyors picked a hill with a steep drop facing a wide plateau and a deep, sheltered bay. The settlement was given the Spanish name Puerto de la Princesa, meaning Port of the Princess, in honor of Infanta Eulalia of Spain, daughter of Queen Isabella II and Francis, Duke of Cadiz, who was born in 1864. Before the Spanish laid out the town, the area was already home to indigenous Palaweno groups, including the Tagbanwa and the Batak in the interior.

By May 1872, the new town was functioning as the hub of Spanish naval operations in Palawan because the bay was deep enough for warships and protected from open-sea weather. Settlement incentives were used to draw in residents from other parts of the country, and by 1883 the town already had a planned street grid, a hospital, and working port facilities. Spanish records from 1894 list Puerto Princesa among the cleanest and most orderly towns in the colony.

When the Americans took over after 1898, the port and administrative buildings from the Spanish period stayed in use, and the city expanded around them in the early 20th century. Local hero Dr. Higinio Mendoza, who was executed by Japanese forces at Canigaran on January 24, 1944 during World War II, later gave his name to the city's first park. Puerto Princesa kept its role as Palawan's capital through the postwar decades and remains the main entry point most travelers use for the rest of the province today.

place_intro:
Kapitolyo is a small barangay inside Pasig City, on the Ortigas business district side of the metro. It is boxed in by four roads most Manila commuters know: EDSA on one side, Shaw Boulevard up top, Pioneer Street on the west, and Pasig Boulevard at the bottom. Once you turn off any of those, the scale shrinks fast. The streets get narrow, the buildings drop to two or three storeys, and you walk past old family homes, sari-sari stores, and converted houses now operating as cafes.

The neighborhood is built around two main spines, East Capitol Drive and West Capitol Drive, with United Street, Brixton Street, San Francisco Street, and Santa Rosa Street feeding into them. East Capitol Drive skews younger and casual. West Capitol Drive carries heavier sit-down spots and longer-running establishments. Because the lots were planned as a residential subdivision, the blocks are walkable and parking is mostly along the curb.

Officially it is one of Pasig's 30 barangays. In everyday talk Filipinos use Kapitolyo as shorthand for the food-blogger pocket between the old Rizal Provincial Capitol site (now Capitol Commons) and the corporate towers of Ortigas Center.

history.heading: From Capitol Subdivision to Pasig's food-blogger pocket
history.body:
Kapitolyo started in the early 1950s as the Capitol Subdivision, a residential project laid out by Ortigas and Company on land the family owned along what was then the edge of Pasig. It is on record as the first formal subdivision ever opened in Pasig, which is how the area picked up its identity as a planned residential pocket rather than a rural barrio. The original barangay hall was built in 1954 on a lot donated by Ortigas and Company, and the surrounding streets were sold off as single-family lots to middle-class buyers moving out of older Manila districts.

The name itself comes from the Rizal Provincial Capitol building that stood right next to the subdivision. The capitol was constructed in 1962 under Rizal Governor Isidro Rodriguez, on a parcel also donated by the Ortigas family to the provincial government, and was designed by architect Ruperto Gaite. It served as the seat of Rizal province until 2009, when a new capitol opened in Antipolo, and the move was formalized in 2020. After the capitol was vacated, the land reverted to Ortigas and Company under the original deed of donation and was redeveloped starting 2011 into the mixed-use Capitol Commons.

For most of the late 20th century Kapitolyo stayed quiet and residential, with long-time families living in the same houses for decades. The shift into a food district happened gradually from the 2010s onward, when homeowners along East and West Capitol Drive began converting their ground floors and garages into restaurants, cafes, and bars. Because the lots were originally homes, most of the dining spots are still small, owner-run, and packed close together, which is why Filipino food bloggers and Pasig locals treat the whole barangay as one walkable strip.

place_intro:
Pasay City sits at the southern edge of Metro Manila, between Manila to the north, Makati to the east, Paranaque to the south, and Manila Bay to the west. It is a highly urbanized city in the National Capital Region with a 2024 population of around 453,186, and one of the smallest in Metro Manila by land area. For most Filipino travelers, Pasay is the city you land in first, since Ninoy Aquino International Airport sits inside its boundaries.

The city splits into three zones. The urban core covers about 5.5 square kilometers, laced by Roxas Boulevard along the bay, EDSA on the east, Taft Avenue, and Gil Puyat (Buendia). The Civil Aeronautics Administration complex of roughly 9.5 square kilometers holds NAIA Terminals 1 to 4 and Villamor Air Base. The reclaimed land along Manila Bay, around 4 square kilometers, is Bay City, home to SM Mall of Asia and Aseana City.

Administratively, Pasay has two legislative districts, 20 zones, and 200-plus barangays identified by number, not name. Older residential pockets sit near Libertad and Taft, hotel strips line Roxas Boulevard, casino-resort towers rise in Entertainment City south of MOA, and an airport-hotel belt wraps NAIA and Newport.

history.heading: From Namayan barangay to Pineda, Rizal City, and Pasay
history.body:
Long before the Spaniards arrived, the area now called Pasay was part of the pre-colonial polity of Namayan along the southern shore of Manila Bay. Local tradition links the name to Dayang-dayang Pasay, a noblewoman of Namayan whose territory, according to oral history, was donated to Spanish friars at her death. Other accounts trace the name to the plant pasaw, but the Dayang-dayang Pasay version is the one the city government itself keeps in its official history.

Under Spanish rule the settlement was folded into Malate and later attached to Paranaque. In 1862, prominent residents petitioned to separate from Paranaque and run their own civil and religious affairs. On December 2, 1863, on the recommendation of Manila Archbishop Gregorio Meliton Martinez Santa Cruz, the town was granted its own municipal charter under the name Pineda, in honor of Don Cornelio Pineda, a Spanish horticulturist who had championed the community. December 2 is still observed locally as the foundation date.

The town reverted to Pasay in the early American period and joined the Province of Rizal. After World War II, President Manuel Roxas signed Republic Act No. 183 on June 21, 1947, converting the municipality into a chartered city and renaming it Rizal City after Jose Rizal. The new name never stuck with residents, and on June 7, 1950, Republic Act No. 437 officially changed Rizal City back to Pasay City.

Modern Pasay was shaped after that. Manila International Airport (renamed Ninoy Aquino International Airport in 1987) anchored the southern half of the city. Manila Bay reclamation produced Bay City, where SM Mall of Asia opened in 2006, and PAGCOR's Entertainment City masterplan brought the integrated resorts now sitting south of MOA.

place_intro:
Dagupan is a coastal independent component city in Pangasinan, Ilocos Region (Region I), sitting along the southern shore of the Lingayen Gulf in northwestern Luzon. It is about 14 kilometers east of Lingayen (the provincial capital) and roughly 220 kilometers north of Manila, which makes it a common jump-off point for trips into Pangasinan or up to La Union. It belongs to the 4th congressional district.

The city covers around 4,008 hectares per the City Assessor and is politically subdivided into 31 barangays (locally called districts), each made up of puroks and a few sitios. The terrain is flat coastal lowland crisscrossed by seven rivers and 14 creeks. Climate is Koppen Am (tropical monsoon) with a pronounced dry season from November to April.

The downtown core runs along A.B. Fernandez Avenue, where the Dagupan City Hall Complex and Mayor's Office sit, with Perez Boulevard as the main commercial corridor of banks, retail, hospitals, and pharmacies. Coastal barangays like Bonuan Gueset face the gulf, while inland districts hold the regional banking and government offices that draw daytime commuters from across Pangasinan.

history.heading: From Bacnotan to Nandaragupan to Dagupan City
history.body:
The area that is now Dagupan was first named Bacnotan by Augustinian missionaries a few years after the Spanish encomienda system was established in Pangasinan in 1583. Under that early colonial setup, Bacnotan was already recognized as one of the largest trading areas in the Alcaldia Mayor and across the wider Ilocos Region. Oral and written traditions also trace some of the earliest settlers to Flores Island in the Lesser Sunda Islands of the Indonesian archipelago, citing the close similarity between their dialect and the Pangasinan language.

The turning point came in 1660 with the revolt led by Andres Malong, a local chief who pushed back against the forced labor imposed by Spanish authorities. To mark the uprising and the gathering of rebel forces, the town was renamed Nandaragupan, a Pangasinan word meaning where people meet. By 1780 the name had been simplified to Dagupan, which is the form that stuck through the rest of the Spanish, American, and Commonwealth periods.

Cityhood came after World War II. On June 20, 1947, President Manuel Roxas signed Republic Act No. 170, the City Charter of Dagupan, authored by Speaker Eugenio Perez of Pangasinan. That charter formally converted the old town into a chartered city and set the foundation for the regional commercial role Dagupan still plays today as the financial and government services hub of the province.

place_intro:
Cagayan de Oro is the capital of Misamis Oriental and the regional center of Northern Mindanao (Region X), sitting on a narrow coastal plain along Macajalar Bay on the northern coast of Mindanao. The Cagayan de Oro River splits the city into two congressional districts: the 1st District (West) holds 24 barangays including Carmen, Bulua, Kauswagan, Iponan, Patag, and Lumbia, while the 2nd District (East) holds 56 barangays, among them Lapasan, Gusa, Cugman, Macasandig, Puerto, Bugo, and the 40 numbered Poblacion barangays of the old downtown.

The layout follows the river and the bay. Downtown (Divisoria, Poblacion, and Gaston Park beside St. Augustine Cathedral) sits on the coastal flat at roughly 10 meters above sea level. Uptown stretches inland along Carmen and Patag toward Lumbia, climbing onto a higher escarpment. Lapasan runs east as the main commercial strip toward Cugman and Bugo. Of the 80 barangays, 57 are urbanized and 23 are rural.

For DIY travelers: Poblacion and Divisoria for old-city walking, Lapasan and Limketkai for malls and transit, Carmen and Patag for uptown, and the upstream Cagayan River corridor for the whitewater stretch the city is known for.

history.heading: From Himologan to the City of Golden Friendship
history.body:
Long before any Spanish ship arrived, the area was inhabited by a Lumad and Visayan-descended people who called their fortified hilltop settlement Himologan, located about eight kilometers upriver from the present downtown (the Huluga Open Site is the accepted archaeological match). The lowland along the river was known as Kalambagohan, after the lambago trees that lined the banks. The community was animist but paid tribute to the Sultanate of Maguindanao to the south.

In 1622, two Augustinian Recollect missionaries, Fray Juan de San Nicolas and Fray Francisco de la Madre de Dios, reached Himologan and made contact with Datu Salangsang, the local chief. Four years later, in 1626, Fray Agustín de San Pedro convinced Datu Salangsang to move the settlement down from the hilltop to the lowland along the Cagayan River, on what is now Gaston Park beside St. Augustine Cathedral. Fray de San Pedro then fortified the relocated village against raids tied to Sultan Kudarat of Maguindanao, and that riverside spot became the seed of the modern city.

Through the Spanish and American colonial periods the settlement was simply called Cagayan, and the suffix 'de Oro' (of gold) was later attached in reference to the gold panned and mined in the hinterland barrios upstream, a fact already noted by Spanish chroniclers from the 1500s. The community was elevated to a chartered city on June 15, 1950, when President Elpidio Quirino signed Republic Act No. 521. Today Cagayan de Oro is the regional center for Northern Mindanao and the 10th most populous city in the country, with a 2024 census population of 741,617.

place_intro:
Malolos is the capital city of Bulacan province in Central Luzon, about 33 kilometers north of Metro Manila and reachable through NLEX exits at Tabang or Marilao. It is a coastal component city facing Manila Bay to the south, with Plaridel to the north, Calumpit to the northwest, Bulakan to the southeast, Guiguinto to the east, and Paombong to the west. The city covers roughly 67.25 square kilometers across 51 barangays, with a 2020 census population of 261,189.

The character of Malolos splits into two layers. The old downtown, called the Malolos Historic Town Center or the Kamestisuhan and Pariancillo District, is a walkable grid of narrow streets lined with 19th-century ancestral houses, the Malolos Cathedral, and the Barasoain Church. Outside the heritage core, the city stretches into commercial strips along MacArthur Highway, the Bulacan Capitol grounds, Bulacan State University, and newer subdivisions feeding the Manila commute.

For a DIY traveler, Malolos works as a half-day or full-day base. You can do the heritage loop on foot, take a tricycle to the Capitol area, then ride a jeep toward the fishpond barangays near the bay for the rural side.

history.heading: From 1580 Augustinian Town to Capital of the First Philippine Republic
history.body:
Malolos was established as an independent town on June 11, 1580, when it was separated from the Alcaldia de Calumpit and accepted as an Augustinian priory at a council held in the Tondo Convent. Fray Matheo de Mendoza was installed as its first minister. Before that, the area was a small riverside settlement that Spanish missionaries gradually expanded by clearing forests and organizing the population around a parish church. The name Malolos is commonly traced to the Tagalog word paluslos, meaning downwards, reportedly misheard by early Spanish missionaries from locals describing the flow of the river.

During the Spanish period, Malolos became the encomienda of a Spaniard named Tirado, with about 900 tributes recorded. Through the 19th century, wealthy Chinese mestizo families settled along Calle Pariancillo in the barrio of Santo Nino, building the ancestral homes that now define the Kamestisuhan District. The Barasoain Church, formally the Our Lady of Mount Carmel Parish, was built in 1888 and named after a town in Spain the missionaries thought the area resembled.

The defining chapter came at the end of the Spanish era. On September 15, 1898, the Malolos Congress opened inside Barasoain Church, and on January 23, 1899, the First Philippine Republic was inaugurated there under President Emilio Aguinaldo. The Malolos Constitution drafted at that congress is recognized as the first democratic constitution in Asia. On November 4, 1999, Republic Act 8754 was signed converting the municipality into a component city, and on October 8, 2002, after a Commission on Elections recount, the cityhood ratification was upheld. The National Historical Institute had earlier declared the downtown a National Historical Landmark and Heritage Town on August 15, 2001.

place_intro:
Tarlac is a landlocked province in the Central Luzon region (Region III) of the Philippines, sitting roughly at the center of the Luzon central plains. It is bordered by Pangasinan on the north, Nueva Ecija on the east, Pampanga on the south, and Zambales on the west. The province covers about 3,053.60 square kilometers and is split into three congressional districts, 17 municipalities, and one component city (Tarlac City, the provincial capital), with 511 barangays in total.

The terrain is mostly flat. About 75 percent of the province is plains planted to rice and sugarcane, while the western side turns hilly and slightly mountainous. A portion of Mount Pinatubo sits inside Bamban and Capas, the two southwestern towns most affected by the 1991 eruption.

Tarlac is widely called the "Melting Pot of Central Luzon" because four ethnolinguistic groups live side by side: Kapampangans in the southern towns, Ilocanos in the northern towns, Pangasinenses near the Pangasinan border, and Tagalogs. Tarlac City, sitting in the middle along MacArthur Highway and the NLEX-SCTEX-TPLEX corridor, is where all four mix.

history.heading: How Tarlac became a province
history.body:
Before it was a province, the land that is now Tarlac was thickly forested and roamed by Aeta groups, regarded as among the earliest settlers of the area. The name Tarlac itself comes from an Aeta word for a tall talahib-type weed called tarlak; the area around the present capital was described as matarlak or malatarlak, meaning thick with tarlak grass. During most of the Spanish period, the territory was not its own province at all. The northern towns were administered as part of Pangasinan, while the southern towns were administered as part of Pampanga.

Tarlac was the last province in Central Luzon to be carved out by the Spanish colonial government. It was inaugurated as a regular province on May 28, 1873, by slicing the southern towns away from Pangasinan and the northern towns away from Pampanga and fusing them into one new political unit. This is why the modern province still reads as a mix of Ilocano-speaking towns up north and Kapampangan-speaking towns down south. The May 28 founding date is still the anchor of the annual provincial celebration, now branded as the Melting Pot Festival.

When the Philippine Revolution broke out in 1896, Tarlac was one of the first eight provinces to rise against Spain, which is why its sun ray appears on the Philippine flag. In 1899, during the First Philippine Republic under Emilio Aguinaldo, Tarlac City briefly served as the seat of the revolutionary government before the capital moved further north. From the American period onward, Tarlac grew as a sugar and rice province, with large haciendas (most famously Hacienda Luisita in Tarlac City) and sugar centrals shaping the towns, while Victoria became known for muscovado.

place_intro:
General Santos City, shortened to GenSan by locals, sits at the southern tip of Mindanao along Sarangani Bay in the Soccsksargen region. It is wedged between South Cotabato and Sarangani Province, with Polomolok and Malungon to the north, Alabel to the east, Maasim to the south, and T'boli to the west. The city center is at roughly 6.1 North, 125.2 East, about 18 meters above sea level, and is a 1st class highly urbanized city.

The city has 26 barangays, but travelers mostly hear three names: Dadiangas, Buayan, and Lagao. Dadiangas is the downtown core, split into East, North, South, and West. Dadiangas East is the tightest grid for shopping, bounded by the National Highway, Ireneo Santiago Boulevard, Sergio Osmena Street, and Pendatun Avenue. Buayan covers the eastern side near the old airfield and river. Lagao sits about 3.5 km northeast of Dadiangas.

Most arrivals come through General Santos International Airport in Barangay Tambler, which also hosts the General Santos Fish Port Complex on a 32-hectare site. The fish port is the second largest in the Philippines after Navotas, and the airport runway, at 3,227 meters, is among the longest in the country.

history.heading: From Dadiangas Settlement to Tuna-Capital City
history.body:
Long before the city existed under its current name, the area was home to the Blaan people, who called the place Dadiangas after the Ziziphus spina-christi tree that grew across the flat plain where downtown now stands. The eastern side of today's city was historically known as Buayan, named after the Buayan River where, according to local accounts, elders warned children away by shouting about crocodiles in the water. Makar formed the western portion. Dadiangas itself served as the seat of government for the old Municipality of Buayan before the modern settlement era began.

The modern founding is tied to a specific date and a specific person. On February 27, 1939, a first group of 62 settlers from Luzon, led by General Paulino Santos, landed on the shores of Sarangani Bay. They came under the National Land Settlement Administration (NLSA), a program of President Manuel L. Quezon's government that distributed Mindanao land to peasants from Central Luzon. In March 1939, the first formal settlement was established in Alagao, now known as Barangay Lagao, which became the administration center for the NLSA Koronadal project.

The place was renamed General Santos in June 1954 in honor of Paulino Santos. On July 8, 1968, Republic Act No. 5412, authored by Congressman James L. Chiongbian, converted the Municipality of General Santos into a city. From there the city grew around three anchor zones: Dadiangas as the commercial core, Lagao as the older administrative belt, and Tambler as the port and airport gateway. The Tuna Festival held every September is the clearest civic expression of the city's identity today.

place_intro:
Venice Grand Canal is a Venetian themed lifestyle mall inside the McKinley Hill township in Taguig, Metro Manila. The registered address is McKinley Hill Garden Villas, Cluster B, Upper McKinley Road, Pinagsama, Taguig, on the 50 hectare McKinley Hill development run by Megaworld Corporation. From Lawton Avenue you go up the hill past the Chinese International School and the Korean embassy, and the complex is the part with the bell tower poking above the rooflines.

The layout is built around a 200 meter artificial canal that runs through the open air center, with gondola docks at a Rialto Bridge replica and a smaller Ponte de Amore crossing. The retail block is three upper floors plus one basement, wrapped around the water so the shopfronts look down on the gondolas. The ground level plaza is Venice Piazza, modeled on Piazza San Marco, with a replica of St. Mark's Campanile anchoring the skyline.

Inside the township the mall acts as the social square for the surrounding condominium clusters, the office towers under Megaworld Lifestyle Malls, and the student crowd from Enderun Colleges. It is pet friendly, the walkways are cobblestoned, and the canal lights up at night.

history.heading: From McKinley Hill expansion to a Venetian canal mall
history.body:
Venice Grand Canal Mall opened to the public on September 18, 2015. It was developed by Megaworld Corporation, the Andrew Tan led property group, and is managed under its Megaworld Lifestyle Malls retail arm. The land itself is part of the McKinley Hill township, a 50 hectare master planned community that Megaworld carved out of the larger Fort Bonifacio area south of Bonifacio Global City. McKinley Hill was designed from the start as a globally themed enclave, with Spanish and Italian architectural cues running through the residential clusters, the office towers, and the embassy row, so the Venetian canal concept tied the township's retail piece into the same look.

The Italian design work was led by the Rome based architectural firm Paolo Marioni Architetto, which is why the campanile, the arched windows, and the bridges read closer to central Venice than the usual mall pastiche. The 200 meter long, 15 meter wide canal was engineered as the centerpiece, with the Rialto Bridge replica housing the gondola stations and the open air Venice Piazza built as a stand in for Piazza San Marco. In 2017 the project won Best Retail Architectural Design at the Philippine Property Awards.

The complex is tied to the older Venice Luxury Residences condominium cluster that Megaworld had already turned over on the same hillside, so by 2015 the residential side of the Venetian theme was already lived in. The mall is newer than it looks, the township around it gives it the European street feel, and Megaworld is the single entity behind both.

place_intro:
SM Seaside City Cebu sits on the South Road Properties (SRP), a 300-hectare reclaimed strip off the southern coast of Cebu City, near the barangays of Mambaling, Inayawan, and Pasil. It faces the Mactan Channel. From Cebu City proper you reach it in about 20 minutes by car along the Cebu South Coastal Road, and Mactan-side travelers can come in via the Cebu-Cordova Link Expressway (CCLEX).

The mall is circular, shaped after a nautilus shell, with concentric arcs fanning out from a central court. The layout is built around a few landmarks: the central courtyard with its 150-meter bell tower, the Sky Park roof deck, the cinema cluster, the SM Bowling and Amusement Center, and an Olympic-size ice skating rink near the food court. The CUBE sculpture at the facade (an anagram of CEBU) is the first-timer photo spot.

The rest of SRP is still filling in around it. Neighbors on the same reclamation block include South Coast City (a 26-hectare SM Prime and Ayala Land project launched in 2020) and Filinvest's 48-hectare City di Mare, so the area reads more like a planned waterfront district than a roadside mall.

history.heading: From reclaimed seabed to Cebu's nautilus-shaped anchor mall
history.body:
The land under SM Seaside City did not exist a generation ago. In the late 1980s, then-Cebu City mayor Tomas Osmena proposed reclaiming a stretch of the Mactan Channel between mainland Cebu and Kawit Island. The plan was formally approved in 1991 as part of the Metro Cebu Development Project, a study financed by Japanese Official Development Assistance. Civil works began in 1997, were bankrolled by a 12.315 billion yen loan from the Japan Bank for International Cooperation, and wrapped up in 2004 after roughly 9 million cubic meters of quarried fill, mostly diorite from upland Naga, Cebu, were laid down. The result was the 300-hectare South Road Properties.

SM Prime Holdings entered the picture in 2009, submitting a formal offer to buy 28 hectares of SRP and proposing a 20 billion peso, 15-year mixed-use development anchored by a mall. The Cebu City government approved the sale in 2010. On April 12, 2011, SM Prime held the ground-breaking at the corner of F. Vestil Street and the Cebu South Coastal Road.

The mall was designed by Arquitectonica, the Miami-based firm, with W.V. Coscolluela as architect of record and Gardens by Sanders handling landscape. Because the site faces the Mactan Channel, the architects modeled the building on a nautilus shell, with its logarithmic spiral guiding the concentric arcs of the floor plate. SM Seaside City Cebu opened to the public on November 27, 2015, exactly 22 years after SM City Cebu opened in 1993. As of 2025 it is reported as the second largest mall in Cebu and among the largest in the Philippines, with a gross floor area of about 470,486 square meters.
history.citation_label: Wikipedia: SM Seaside City and South Road Properties

place_intro:
Ayala Triangle Gardens is a two-hectare landscaped public park in the middle of the Makati Central Business District. The park is shaped like its name: a triangle bordered by three of Makati's busiest roads. Ayala Avenue runs along the southwest, Paseo de Roxas along the north-northeast, and Makati Avenue forms the east-southeast edge. The address is Paseo de Roxas corner Makati Avenue and Ayala Avenue, Makati City 1226, south of Salcedo Village on the border of Barangay Bel-Air with San Lorenzo and Urdaneta.

The grounds are designed around paths cutting diagonally across the triangle, with over a hundred trees including rain trees, golden palms, fire trees, kamuning, and podocarpus. Office workers use the paths as a shortcut between Ayala Avenue and Makati Avenue. The park is owned and managed by Ayala Land.

The triangle is ringed by well-known Makati buildings. Along Ayala Avenue stand the old Makati Stock Exchange Center and Tower One and Exchange Plaza, a 35-storey, 160-meter office tower. The old Nielson Tower, the original control tower, still stands inside the park. At the Makati Avenue and Paseo de Roxas corner sit Ayala Triangle Gardens Tower One and Tower Two.

history.heading: From Nielson Field airport to Makati's green triangle
history.body:
Long before it was a park, the land was part of the Hacienda San Pedro de Macati owned by the Zobel de Ayala family. In 1937, a British businessman and aviation enthusiast named Laurie Nielson leased 42 hectares of swampland from the first Don Enrique Zobel to build an airport and aviation school. Construction used about 1,000 workers and 80,000 gallons of asphalt over six months. Nielson Field opened in July 1937 and was hailed at the time as the largest and best-equipped airport in Asia, serving as Manila's main civilian and commercial airfield before the Second World War.

The airport had two runways. Those runways are still here today, just not as runways anymore. After the war, Nielson Field was decommissioned in 1948 and air operations were transferred to Nichols Field in what is now Pasay and Paranaque. The land returned to the Ayala family, who then redeveloped it into the Makati Central Business District. The two old runways were paved over and became Ayala Avenue and Paseo de Roxas, the same streets that now bound the triangle. The original Nielson Field control tower was kept and later restored, eventually housing the Filipinas Heritage Library and, in 2014, the Blackbird restaurant.

For decades after Makati's CBD rose around it, the triangle itself was mostly closed to the public and used as a parking area and service ground for the surrounding offices. Ayala Land redeveloped the parcel into a public landscaped park, taking design cues from London's Hyde Park, and Ayala Triangle Gardens opened to the public on November 19, 2009. The Shops at Ayala Triangle Gardens, built into the Tower Two podium at the Makati Avenue and Paseo de Roxas corner, came later as part of the second tower's development by Ayala Land.
history.citation_label: Wikipedia: Ayala Triangle Gardens and Nielson Field

place_intro:
BF Homes Parañaque, officially Barangay BF Homes, sits at the southern end of Parañaque City in Metro Manila. It is the largest of Parañaque's 16 barangays at around 7.67 square kilometers, on land that used to be farmland in old Barrio San Dionisio plus parcels reaching into Las Piñas and Muntinlupa. It is bordered by Barangay San Antonio to the north, San Isidro to the west, the South Luzon Expressway (SLEX) to the east, and Las Piñas and Muntinlupa to the south.

The subdivision is laid out in numbered phases, with Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, and Phase 5 the most referenced. Phase 1 is the oldest and most built-up. Aguirre Avenue runs through the middle as the main commercial spine, backed by El Grande Avenue, Presidents Avenue, and Concha Cruz Drive.

Inside, the feel is upper middle class suburban: low-rise houses on wide blocks and a slower pace than the EDSA strip up north. Many residents commute to Makati, BGC, and Ortigas, so weekdays stay quiet and weekends fill the avenues. Philippine media call it one of the biggest residential subdivisions in Asia, and for a DIY traveler it is a neighborhood to wander rather than a single sight.

history.heading: From San Dionisio farmland to Aguirre's subdivision
history.body:
BF Homes Parañaque was developed by Tomas Aguirre, the same Aguirre family behind the Agencia de Empeños de Aguirre pawnshop business and the founder of Banco Filipino in 1964. Using capital tied to the family business, Aguirre put together roughly 765 hectares of agricultural land, most of it from Barrio San Dionisio in Parañaque, plus adjoining parcels in Las Piñas and Muntinlupa. Before the subdivision existed, the area was farmland on the southern edge of what was then a much smaller Metro Manila.

Ground broke on the BF Homes project in 1968, and lots went on sale that same year. By December 1969 the subdivision held its first annual homeowners meeting at the clubhouse on 37 Pilar Banzon Street, and the development was fully operational by early 1970. It was rolled out alongside sister BF Homes projects in Quezon City, Caloocan, and Naga, but the Parañaque tract became the largest of the group.

The Aguirre-led era ended in the mid-1980s. In 1985 Tomas Aguirre fell out of favor with the Marcos government and Banco Filipino was shut down on grounds of insolvency, which dragged down the developer entity BFHI as well. By March 1989, management of the subdivision was handed over from BFHI to the residents themselves through the United BF Homeowners Association Incorporated (UBFHAI), which still oversees the community today.

place_intro:
Nuvali is a large mixed-use estate in southern Calabarzon, straddling parts of Sta. Rosa, Cabuyao, and Calamba in Laguna. It sits about an hour south of Metro Manila via SLEX and the Sta. Rosa exit, which is why weekend traffic from QC, Makati, and BGC pours in here on Saturdays. The estate covers roughly 2,290 hectares of rolling former sugar land, repurposed by Ayala Land into what the company markets as the country's first eco-city.

The layout is not a single strip but a network of pocket districts linked by a long internal road system and a 17-kilometer buffer of preserved green and forest zone. The most visited core for outsiders is Solenad, the lakeside lifestyle strip with the koi pond and the Ayala Malls Solenad complex. Around it sit residential villages by Ayala Land Premier, Alveo, Avida, and Amaia, including Abrio, Santierra, Treveia, Venare, Elaro, and Luscara, plus schools, hospitals, and office parks.

For a DIY visitor, the mental map is simple: Solenad is the food and shopping anchor, the Wildlife and Bird Sanctuary is the trail and birding zone with view decks, and the rest is mostly gated residential. Distances inside Nuvali are larger than Waze makes them look.

history.heading: From Canlubang sugar land to Ayala Land's eco-city
history.body:
The ground Nuvali stands on has a long pre-Ayala history. In 1912 a California-based group called Ehrman Trust bought up large tracts in barangay Canlubang to enter the sugar business, and the Canlubang sugar mill began operations on December 1, 1914. Ehrman Trust later hired a young lawyer, Jose Yulo, as legal counsel and caretaker of the estate in 1927. Vicente Madrigal bought Canlubang in 1941, then sold it to Don Jose Yulo on September 16, 1948, together with the sugar estate. For decades after that, the area was known as Hacienda Yulo and stayed agricultural under Yulo family companies.

The shift to a planned township came through a partnership between Ayala Land and the Yulo family, specifically the Yulo-owned San Cristobal Realty Development Corporation, to convert about 1,700 hectares of former sugarcane land into a master-planned community. Ayala Land entered pre-selling for the residential components in October 2007, and on September 16, 2008, Ayala Land employees held a tree-planting on a site now inside Nuvali. The estate was formally launched in 2009 as a 17-kilometer-long green development spanning Sta. Rosa, Cabuyao, and Calamba.

From that 2009 launch, Nuvali expanded steadily. By 2019 it was reported at around 2,290 hectares, and by 2024 it had grown to roughly 2,500 hectares with over 20,000 residential units built out by Ayala Land Premier, Alveo, Avida, and Amaia. One side note worth knowing: the Republic Wakepark, a 4-hectare cable wakeboarding lake inside Nuvali, operated from 2012 until it closed in 2021 due to financial reasons and the COVID-19 pandemic, so older blogs that list it as a Nuvali activity are out of date.
history.citation_label: Nuvali (Wikipedia) and Ayala Land Investor Relations

place_intro:
Bacolod is the capital of Negros Occidental and the largest city on the northwestern coast of Negros Island, in Western Visayas (Region VI). It sits between the Guimaras Strait to the west and inland sugarcane country to the east, bordered by Talisay, Silay, Victorias, Murcia, and Bago City. As a highly urbanized city, it is governed independently from the province.

The city covers about 162.67 square kilometers across 61 barangays, with a 2020 census population of 600,783. The street grid is anchored on the downtown core along Rizal Street, where Bacolod Public Plaza sits across from San Sebastian Cathedral, with the old City Hall about 300 meters away and the Central Market roughly a hundred meters south. Newer townships like Megaworld's Upper East have risen on the east side, on land that used to belong to the Bacolod-Murcia Milling Company.

Bacolod is nicknamed the City of Smiles and carries the older tag of Sugar Bowl of the Philippines from its role at the center of the Negros sugar industry. Burgos Street, once called Millionaire's Row, is still lined with old sugar-era mansions of the hacendero families.

history.heading: From San Sebastian de Magsungay to chartered city
history.body:
Bacolod did not begin where the downtown sits today. The original coastal settlement nearby was called San Sebastian de Magsungay. On July 14, 1755, forces under Datu Bantilan of Sulu attacked the village, and the survivors moved inland to a hilly area locally known as bakolod, the Hiligaynon word for hill, which gave the new town its name. The town of Bacolod was formally constituted between 1755 and 1756 on that higher ground.

Under Spanish rule, Bacolod was set up as a parroquia in 1788 under the secular clergy, although it did not get a resident parish priest until 1802. The town's status climbed through the 19th century as the Negros sugar industry grew. In 1846, Bacolod was designated as the capital of the island, and the open square that became the Bacolod Public Plaza took shape as a public space during this mid-19th century period. In 1889, the old Province of Negros was politically split into Occidental Negros and Oriental Negros, and Bacolod was named the capital of Occidental Negros.

The plaza's current musical-themed layout, with its central gazebo inscribed with the names of Western composers, was built in 1927. Just over a decade later, Bacolod was formally inaugurated as a chartered city on October 19, 1938, by virtue of Commonwealth Act No. 404, an event marked by a visit from Commonwealth President Manuel L. Quezon. From that point onward, the sugar-era wealth of the Negros hacenderos kept reshaping the downtown, leaving behind the heritage mansions along Burgos Street that still stand today.

place_intro:
Lucky Chinatown is a lifestyle mall complex along Reina Regente Street in Binondo, Manila, the district that carries the title of the world's oldest Chinatown. It sits inside the grid bounded by Reina Regente, Quintin Paredes, and the back streets feeding into Ongpin, so it works as an anchor tying old Binondo to a newer retail strip. The complex is run by Megaworld Lifestyle Malls and covers about 108,000 square metres of floor area across three to five levels, with parking for around a thousand cars.

The property is not a single box. It is split into separate wings, including the Prosperity Wing and the newer Imperial Wing that opened in 2025, plus the Hotel Lucky Chinatown tower and the Chinatown Museum on the fourth level. Between the blocks runs the open-air Chinatown Walk, a covered promenade lined with Filipino-Chinese specialty stalls meant to feel closer to old Ongpin than to a standard mall.

For a DIY traveller, the layout is useful to know. Reina Regente has the main drop-off, the hotel faces the back of the property, and Chinatown Walk links the wings at ground level. Cabs, jeepneys along Quintin Paredes, and LRT-1 Carriedo station nearby all feed the same block.

history.heading: From two public high schools to the Chinatown Heritage Project
history.body:
The land Lucky Chinatown stands on was not always commercial. Before Megaworld Corporation moved in, the block in Binondo was occupied by two Manila public high schools, Rajah Soliman High School and Jose Abad Santos High School. Megaworld acquired the property in 2008 and folded it into what the company branded as the Chinatown Heritage Project, a redevelopment meant to give Binondo a modern retail anchor while keeping its Filipino-Chinese identity at the centre of the design.

The mall officially opened on August 15, 2012. Then President Benigno Aquino III attended the opening ceremony, which framed the project as a flagship development for the district rather than just another shopping centre. The first phase delivered the main mall and the Chinatown Walk promenade, with the Prosperity Wing handling the bulk of the retail floors.

The complex kept growing after that. On April 10, 2019, Hotel Lucky Chinatown opened on the property as a 93-room hotel tower, giving Binondo a branded in-district accommodation option. A few weeks later, in June 2019, the Chinatown Museum opened on the fourth level of the mall. The museum is recognised by the National Commission for Culture and the Arts and covers the story of Chinese migration to the Philippines and the growth of Binondo itself. In 2025, Megaworld added the Imperial Wing, a three-level retail extension that expanded the footprint further along the block.

place_intro:
Newport Mall sits inside the Newport World Resorts complex in Newport City, a 25-hectare Megaworld Corporation township in Pasay City. The mall faces Newport Boulevard and sits directly across from Ninoy Aquino International Airport Terminal 3, which is why a lot of Filipino travelers swing by either before a flight or right after landing. Beside it is the Villamor Golf Course, and the whole township is wedged between airport land and the older Villamor Air Base perimeter.

The mall is a four-level complex built into the same building as the Resorts World casino, the Newport Performing Arts Theater, and the hotels that anchor the township, including Manila Marriott, Sheraton Manila, Hotel Okura Manila, Hilton Manila, and Holiday Inn Express Newport City. The corridors are wider and quieter than a typical Metro Manila mall, and you can walk from a hotel lobby straight into the shops without stepping outside.

The retail mix leans premium, with boutiques like Swarovski, Rolex, Bvlgari, Salvatore Ferragamo, Charles and Keith, and Bench across the levels, while the 4th level holds the four Newport Cinemas plus access to the Performing Arts Theater for concerts and stage shows.

history.heading: From Villamor Air Base land to a Megaworld township
history.body:
Newport City was carved out of land adjacent to Villamor Air Base, the Philippine Air Force property in Pasay, and was developed by Megaworld Corporation, the Andrew Tan-led real estate group, as a 25-hectare integrated township opposite NAIA Terminal 3. The estate was master-planned to combine residences, hotels, a casino, a performing arts theater, and a retail mall under one walkable footprint, which at the time was a new format for the Philippines.

The anchor of the township is the integrated resort now called Newport World Resorts. It is owned and operated by Travellers International Hotel Group, Inc., a joint venture between Alliance Global Group and Genting Hong Kong. The complex held its soft launch on August 28, 2009 under the original name Resorts World Manila, and Newport Mall opened as the retail wing of that same complex, sharing the building with the casino, the theater, and the first batch of hotels.

For more than a decade the property traded under the Resorts World Manila brand. On July 20, 2022, Alliance Global rebranded the entire complex to Newport World Resorts, and the mall has since been marketed as Newport Mall under Megaworld Lifestyle Malls. The township was named Mixed-Use Development of the Year by the Philippine Retailers Association in 2015, a useful marker of when the wider Newport City buildout, including additional hotels like Sheraton, Hilton, and Okura, was reaching full stride.

place_intro:
Capitol Commons is a 10-hectare mixed-use estate in Barangay Oranbo, Pasig City, sitting just east of the Ortigas Center business district and right beside Barangay Kapitolyo. The block is boxed in by Meralco Avenue on the west, Shaw Boulevard on the south, Camino Verde Road on the east, and Capt. Henry Javier Street on the north, which makes it easy to find whether you come in from EDSA via Shaw or roll down Meralco.

Inside the property, the layout is split between retail, office towers, residences, and open ground. Estancia is the anchor lifestyle mall on the Meralco Avenue side, while the rest of the estate holds Ortigas Land condominium towers (Royalton, Maven, Imperium and others) plus the Estancia Offices block. Around half of the total land is kept as open ground, including the Capitol Commons Park and pedestrian walkways linking the towers to the mall.

For the DIY traveler, Capitol Commons works as a self-contained pocket of Ortigas East. You can park or get dropped off, walk between the mall, the park, and the condo lobbies without crossing a major road, and use it as a base for nearby Kapitolyo and the rest of western Pasig.

history.heading: From Rizal Provincial Capitol to Ortigas Land estate
history.body:
The ground under Capitol Commons was originally part of the Hacienda de Mandaloyon, a 4,033-hectare estate covering parts of Pasig, Mandaluyong, San Juan and Quezon City, which Ortigas & Co. founder Don Francisco Ortigas y Barcinas bought from the Augustinian order during the early American colonial period. The Pasig portion stayed under Ortigas & Co. through most of the 20th century.

In 1950, the Provincial Capitol of Rizal was relocated to this site after Ortigas & Co. donated the land. The capitol building was moved here from its earlier location along the Mariquina (Marikina) River in Barangay Santa Rosa, Pasig, and the new complex anchored the area for close to six decades. The adjoining barangay was eventually named Kapitolyo precisely because of this provincial capitol. Pasig itself was separated from Rizal province and annexed to Metropolitan Manila on November 7, 1975, but the capitol stayed put and continued to serve Rizal from inside the new metropolis.

In 2008, the Rizal provincial government finally moved its seat to Antipolo, into the new capitol near the Ynares Center. With the provincial offices gone, ownership of the old capitol grounds reverted to Ortigas & Co. for redevelopment. The company officially launched the Capitol Commons project on December 14, 2011, announcing a 25-billion-peso investment for a mixed-use estate to be built out over roughly 15 years. Estancia mall opened in 2014 as the retail anchor, and a major Estancia expansion wing opened on November 29, 2019, giving the mall a gross floor area of around 76,000 square meters and cementing Capitol Commons as the current Ortigas East lifestyle hub.

place_intro:
NAIA Terminal 3 sits along Andrews Avenue in Pasay City, Metro Manila, postal address Andrews Avenue, Pasay, 1300. It is the newest and largest of the four passenger terminals at Ninoy Aquino International Airport, the country's primary international gateway, on the eastern side of the airport complex opposite Terminal 1 and Terminal 2.

The terminal covers around 182,500 square meters across four passenger levels, with a separate multi-level parking garage. Arrivals are on the lower level and departures on the upper level, with 24 gates, 20 jet bridges, four remote stands served by apron buses, and 140 check-in counters. A 0.22-kilometer footbridge, the Runway Manila bridge, links the terminal to Newport City and Resorts World Manila across Andrews Avenue.

NAIA Terminal 3 is the main hub for Cebu Pacific, which runs both its domestic and international 5J flights out of the building, and it hosts most foreign carriers serving Manila. Philippine Airlines and AirAsia use their own NAIA terminals, so Terminal 3 reads as a mixed international and domestic facility on the Pasay side of the airport.

history.heading: From PIATCO contract to fully operational terminal
history.body:
The Terminal 3 project was awarded in 1997 to Philippine Air Terminals Company Incorporated, known as PIATCO, a consortium led by Philippine partners Paircargo, Security Bank and Trust Company, Equitable Banking Corporation, Chuah Huh Holdings and Philippine Airport Ground Services, with Germany's Fraport AG holding a 30 percent stake and Japan's Nissho Iwai holding 10 percent. Skidmore, Owings and Merrill of the United States designed the building, and Japanese contractor Takenaka was commissioned by PIATCO in 1998 to construct it. The reported project cost was around US$640 million.

Construction ran from 1997 through 2002, but the terminal sat largely unused for years. In May 2003 the Philippine Supreme Court declared the PIATCO contracts null and void after President Gloria Macapagal Arroyo questioned the agreement, and in December 2004 the Philippine government took over the unfinished terminal. PIATCO took the dispute to the International Chamber of Commerce, while Fraport filed a separate case at the International Center for the Settlement of Investment Disputes. ICSID ruled in favor of the Philippine government in August 2007, and the ICC case became final and executory in favor of the government in January 2012.

Terminal 3 partially opened in July 2008, initially handling a limited set of domestic flights, and was declared fully operational in 2014 once international carriers were moved in. In December 2025, the Public-Private Partnership Center attended the unveiling of new and modernized facilities at Terminal 3 under the New NAIA Infrastructure Corporation rehabilitation program, marking the most recent overhaul of the building since the government takeover.

place_intro:
Camp John Hay sits on the southeastern edge of Baguio City along Loakan Road, at roughly 1,500 meters above sea level inside the pine belt of the Cordillera summer capital. The historical military core covers 246 hectares, and the wider estate stretches across forest preserves, ridge trails and residential zones. Locals treat it as its own district rather than a single attraction.

The property is organized into clear sub-areas. The Historical Core holds the Bell House, the Bell Amphitheater, the Cemetery of Negativism, the Secret Garden and the History Trail. Scout Hill carries the picnic grounds, the Butterfly Sanctuary and the go-kart area, while the 48-hectare golf course occupies the western slope under old-growth pines. The Manor Hotel, Forest Lodge and Ayala Technohub round out the built-up sections.

For DIY travelers, the character is closer to a cool-climate forest park stitched to a heritage zone. Roads curve under tall Benguet pines, and most cabins sit tucked among the trees. Public jeepneys reach the main gate from the central business district, and taxis from Burnham Park usually take 10 to 15 minutes.

history.heading: From 1903 US Rest Camp to a 246-Hectare Heritage Reserve
history.body:
The site was first occupied in 1900 by the United States 48th Infantry under Captain Robert Rudd, who set up Hill Station during the Philippine-American War. On 25 October 1903, US President Theodore Roosevelt signed the executive order that formally reserved 213 hectares as a recreation and rest facility for American troops based in the lowlands. The reservation was named after John Milton Hay, the US Secretary of State at the time, and the cool 5,000-foot elevation was chosen so soldiers could recover from heat and tropical disease.

The land transfer was finalized on 7 October 1910, when the US military paid 150,000 pesos for the property following a US Supreme Court decision. Under Major General J. Franklin Bell, the camp gained much of the infrastructure visitors still see today, including a hydroelectric plant, an 18-hole golf course, an expanded hospital and the residence now called the Bell House. The Bell Amphitheater on the same ridge was also designed by Bell himself. In the early 1980s, John Hightower, then commanding general of John Hay Air Station, set up the Cemetery of Negativism as a symbolic burial ground for negative attitudes.

Camp John Hay was turned over to the Philippine government on 1 July 1991, with Tourism Secretary Rafael Alunan III attending the handover. In 1996, the Bases Conversion and Development Authority leased a 247-hectare portion to Camp John Hay Development Corporation, Fil-Estate Management Inc. and Penta Capital Investment Corp. for a 25-year tourism redevelopment contract. After long disputes, a 2015 arbitral ruling ordered the developer to return the property, the Supreme Court upheld it in October 2024, and BCDA took over operations by January 2025.

place_intro:
Cebu IT Park, also called Asiatown IT Park, is a 27-hectare master-planned business and lifestyle estate inside Barangay Apas in the northern half of Cebu City. It is one of two districts that make up the Cebu Park District, the other being Cebu Business Park near Ayala Center. From the Fuente Osmena rotunda, the park is roughly a 10 to 15 minute Grab ride uphill, with Salinas Drive and Archbishop Reyes Avenue feeding into the property.

Inside the perimeter, the layout follows a grid of wide internal streets ringed by office towers, residential condos, and retail rows. The southern edge is anchored by Ayala Malls Central Bloc, while the eastern stretches hold BPO buildings such as the eBloc towers and several call-centre podiums tied to occupants like JP Morgan Chase, Accenture, and IBM. Garden Bloc, a low-rise restaurant strip, sits in the middle and turns car-free on selected evenings.

For a Filipino traveller staying here, the appeal is that almost everything is walkable, sidewalks are wide and lit, and 24/7 BPO operations mean the area never fully shuts. Condos for short stays cluster around Asia Premier Residences, and the main road loop circles in under 30 minutes on foot.

history.heading: From Lahug Airport runway to Cebu's first IT economic zone
history.body:
The land under Cebu IT Park was, for most of the 20th century, the old Lahug Airport. The airfield opened in 1938 and served as Cebu's main commercial airport until operations were suspended in 1966, when traffic was moved to the new Mactan facility across the channel. The runway stayed in limited use for years afterward, and on 19 February 1981 Pope John Paul II celebrated an open-air Papal Mass on the strip during his Philippine visit, an event many older Cebuanos still mark as the airport's last large public moment.

The site was formally retired in 1989 when it was acquired by Cebu Property Ventures and Development Corporation, a subsidiary of Cebu Holdings, Inc., which is in turn part of the Ayala Land group. Planning for the redevelopment as a technology park ran through the late 1990s. On 6 April 2000 the Philippine Economic Zone Authority board approved the estate as an economic zone, and Presidential Proclamation No. 12, signed on 27 February 2001, designated it an Information Technology Special Economic Zone. This made Cebu IT Park one of the first PEZA-registered IT zones outside Metro Manila.

Ground-up construction followed quickly. eOffice One, the first office building inside the estate, started construction in 2001 and opened in 2002, drawing the early wave of BPO and contact-centre locators. The eBloc office towers came next, with eBloc Tower 2 launched in June 2010 and eBloc Tower 3 announced in 2012. Residential development arrived alongside, with Asia Premier Residences opening in 2011 as the first condominium inside the park. The eOffice One footprint was later cleared to make way for Ayala Malls Central Bloc, which opened in 2019 and is now the estate's main retail anchor.

place_intro:
SM City Calamba sits along the National Road in Barangay Real, Calamba City, Laguna, inside the junction locals call the Calamba City Triangle, where Real Road meets the Manila East Road heading to Pagsanjan. Calamba-bound buses from Buendia and Cubao pass the junction, and jeepneys and UV Express vans from Los Banos, Cabuyao, and Sta. Cruz loop through the crossing fronting the mall.

The complex is a full-size SM Supermall built across a main building and an annex, with three retail floors and a gross floor area of about 66,516 square meters on roughly 60,102 square meters of land. SM Supermarket and SM Department Store anchor the wings, SM Cinemas and SM Cyberzone sit upstairs, and around 150 specialty stores, banks, and service kiosks fill the rest. Surface parking wraps the Real Road side.

In Calamba, the mall works as the de facto town center for the western half of Laguna. The Rizal Shrine and old Poblacion are a short drive north, while the resort belt of Pansol and Bucal sits east toward Los Banos. Day-trippers heading to Pansol hot spring resorts often use SM Calamba as their meet-up and supply stop before going inland.

history.heading: From a Calamba junction lot to SM Prime's 39th mall
history.body:
SM City Calamba opened to the public on October 15, 2010, as the 39th shopping mall of SM Prime Holdings and the third SM Supermall in the province of Laguna, after SM City Sta. Rosa (2006) and SM City San Pablo, which opened in the same year. The launch was timed together with SM City San Pablo, and SM Prime publicly framed the pair as its push to anchor Calabarzon retail south of Metro Manila.

The site chosen was the Calamba City Triangle along the National Road in Barangay Real, a junction lot that for years had served mainly as a transit point for buses and jeepneys turning toward Los Banos, Pagsanjan, and Sta. Cruz.
