SM Prime, the mall arm built on the retail empire that Henry Sy, Sr. started from a shoe store on Carriedo Street in Quiapo in the 1950s, developed the property as a three-level mall with a main building and an annex, with SM Department Store and SM Supermarket as the anchor tenants from day one.

After opening, SM Prime pursued an expansion of the Calamba property in the mid-2010s to add leasable space around the existing structure. The works ran into delays tied to permitting and site preparation. By the late 2010s, SM Prime had also begun planning a separate property in the Turbina area of Calamba, reported as SM Calamba Turbina, as a second mall footprint for the city rather than a pure extension of the Barangay Real site.

place_intro:
Tacloban City sits on the northeastern tip of Leyte Island in Eastern Visayas, fronting Cancabato Bay at the mouth of the San Juanico Strait, the channel that separates Leyte from Samar. The San Juanico Bridge crosses that strait north of the city. Tacloban covers around 201.72 square kilometers and serves as the regional center of Eastern Visayas, the capital of Leyte, and the only Highly Urbanized City in the region.

The city is politically subdivided into 138 barangays grouped into puroks and sitios. Downtown Tacloban hugs the bay, with the old commercial core, port, government offices, and Santo Nino Church clustered near the waterfront where Kabatok and his people first settled. From there, the city stretches inland toward Real Street, V&G Subdivision, Marasbaras, and Fatima Village, while the New Bus Terminal in Abucay handles overland trips.

For a DIY traveler, Tacloban is the practical landing point for trips around Leyte and across to Samar. Daniel Z. Romualdez Airport sits on the same peninsula, ferries use the port, and jeepneys run between downtown, the airport, and nearby Palo where the MacArthur Landing Memorial Park stands.

history.heading: From Kankabatok fishing settlement to Eastern Visayas capital
history.body:
Long before Spanish contact, the area now known as Tacloban was a fishing settlement called Kankabatok, named after a prominent early settler, Kabatok, whose territory hugged the bay that still carries his name (Cancabato Bay). The Jesuits worked as the first evangelizers of Leyte, and when they left in 1768 the Augustinians took over. In 1770, Augustinian missionaries established a visita, or chapel barrio, of Tacloban under the jurisdiction of Palo, turning the old Kankabatok settlement into a Spanish-style town. The present name comes from the bamboo fish-and-crab trap called a taklub that locals used in the shallows, with fishermen saying they were going to tarakluban, the place where the trap was used. Tarakluban shortened over time to Tacloban.

By 1830, Tacloban was already a working trading hub because of its position between Leyte and Samar and its access to the Pacific. On February 26, 1839, Tacloban was definitively separated from Samar and proclaimed the capital of Leyte province. During World War II, after General Douglas MacArthur and Allied forces landed on Red Beach in nearby Palo on October 20, 1944, Tacloban was captured the following day and was used as the temporary capital of the Philippines until Manila was retaken. On June 20, 1952, Republic Act No. 760, signed by President Elpidio Quirino, proclaimed Tacloban a chartered city, with Ildefonso Cinco as the first city mayor when the charter took effect in 1953. On October 4, 2008, President Gloria Macapagal Arroyo proclaimed Tacloban a Highly Urbanized City, a status ratified by voters on December 18, 2008, making it the first HUC in Eastern Visayas.

place_intro:
Tagbilaran City is the capital of Bohol province and sits on the southwestern coast of Bohol Island in Central Visayas (Region VII). It is a component city covering about 36.50 square kilometers, with a 13 km coastline facing Panglao Island. The city is roughly 630 km southeast of Manila and 72 km south of Cebu City, which makes it the main jump-off point for travelers coming into Bohol by sea or by road from Panglao International Airport.

The city is laid out as a long strip pinned between two low hills, Banat-i (about 145 meters) on the north and Elley Hull (about 100 meters) on the south, with the seaport on the western side. It shares land borders with Cortes, Corella, and Baclayon. Two bridges link Tagbilaran to Panglao Island, one at Sitio Ubos in Poblacion I crossing to Totolan in Dauis, and a second from Mansasa to Poblacion, Dauis.

Tagbilaran has 15 barangays, 12 classified as urban. The downtown core sits in the Poblacion barangays, Cogon is the most populous and serves as the commercial belt along CPG Avenue, and Dao on the northern end holds the Integrated Bus Terminal for trips to Carmen, Jagna, and Ubay. The city belongs to the 1st legislative district of Bohol.

history.heading: From Bool Kingdom to Bohol's capital city
history.body:
The settlement that became Tagbilaran traces back to the pre-colonial Bool Kingdom on the southwestern shore of Bohol Island, recorded as early as the 15th century. The commonly cited origin of the name is the local contraction Tagubilaan, from tagu (to hide) and Bilaan, referring to a marauding group, describing a settlement shielded by Panglao Island from raiders coming through the channel. A separate tradition derives the name from Tinabilan, meaning screened, again pointing to the natural cover provided by Panglao on the southwest.

San Jose de Tagbilaran was formally established as a town on 9 February 1742 by General Francisco Antonio Calderon de la Barca, the Military Governor of the Visayas Islands, who carved it out of the older town of Baclayon. The new town was placed under the patronage of Saint Joseph the Worker. For more than two centuries afterward, Tagbilaran functioned as a municipality and the seat of civil and church administration on the western side of Bohol.

In 1965, the three congressmen of Bohol sponsored a bill to convert the municipality into a city. On 18 June 1966, President Ferdinand Marcos signed Republic Act No. 4660, the City Charter of Tagbilaran. The charter set 1 July 1966 as the date the City of Tagbilaran would formally come into existence, which is still observed locally as Charter Day. Since then Tagbilaran has remained the capital of Bohol and the main entry point for ferry traffic from Cebu and connecting transport to Panglao and the rest of the province.

place_intro:
Naga City sits in the southeastern part of Luzon, near the geographic middle of the Bicol Peninsula, which is why locals call it the Heart of Bicol. It is an independent component city inside the province of Camarines Sur, drawn within the province on the map but running its own city government. The city covers about 77.40 square kilometers along the Naga River, at the point where the Naga and Bikol Rivers meet.

Politically, Naga is split into 27 barangays. The older core is Downtown Naga, also called Centro, in the southern section where Plaza Quezon, Plaza Rizal, and Plaza Quince Martires line up along the original colonial street grid. North of Centro is the University Belt, where Ateneo de Naga, the University of Nueva Caceres, and Universidad de Sta. Isabel cluster together.

A second commercial zone, Central Business District 2, runs along Panganiban Drive and Roxas, Ninoy, and Cory avenues on the east side, where the malls, the bus terminal, and the Camarines Sur Industrial and Technological Park sit. Quieter residential pockets like Concepcion Pequena and Bagumbayan Sur lie between the two business districts.

history.heading: From Nueva Caceres pueblo to chartered city of Naga
history.body:
Naga began as a native settlement on the Naga River long before the Spaniards arrived, and its name is traced to the narra tree, which the Bicolanos called naga. In 1575, by order of Spanish Governor-General Francisco de Sande, Captain Pedro de Chaves founded a Spanish settlement next to the existing native village and called it Ciudad de Nueva Caceres, in honor of Sande, who was a native of Caceres in Spain. Over time, the Spanish ciudad and the native pueblo of Naga merged into a single community known as Nueva Caceres, governed by its own ayuntamiento and cabildo under Spanish law. This made it one of the original Spanish royal cities in the colonial Philippines, alongside Manila, Cebu, and Iloilo.

Through the Spanish colonial centuries, Nueva Caceres served as the seat of the Diocese of Caceres and grew into the center of trade, schooling, and religion for the whole Bicol Region. In 1919, the city dropped its Spanish colonial name by law and reverted to its older, native name, Naga. The modern chartered city was created on 18 June 1948, when President Elpidio Quirino signed Republic Act No. 305, the Charter of the City of Naga, with Representatives Juan Q. Miranda, Tomas Clemente, and Toribio Perez, and Senator Vicente Madrigal of the 1st Congress, plus Secretary of Agriculture Mariano Garchitorena, on hand for the signing. Six months later, on 15 December 1948, the new city government of Naga was formally inaugurated, and that date is still observed locally as the Naga City Charter Anniversary.

place_intro:
Arcovia City is a 12.3-hectare master-planned township in Barangay Ugong, Pasig, sitting along Eulogio Rodriguez Jr. Avenue, the stretch most locals just call C-5. It runs alongside the Marikina River east of Ortigas Center, about 3.2 kilometers down C-5 from Eastwood City, the older sister township also built by Megaworld. Coming from BGC or Makati, C-5 drops you straight at the gate.

The layout is the standard Megaworld live-work-play setup in a flatter, riverside footprint. Residential towers like 18 Avenue de Triomphe, Altea, Benissa, and the upcoming Arcovia Park Place sit beside the One Paseo office tower and a retail strip called Arcovia Parade. The Arco de Emperador, a large archway unveiled in February 2019, marks the entry off C-5 and is the easiest landmark to give a Grab driver. Parts of the township were registered as a PEZA Information Technology Park in April 2024, which is why a steady BPO crowd fills the area on weekdays.

For a DIY traveler, the practical character of Arcovia City is compact and walkable. Everything sits inside one block, so you can park once at Landers or the Parade and cover the whole strip on foot.

history.heading: From Ajinomoto factory to riverside township
history.body:
Before any of the towers went up, the land that Arcovia City now occupies was an Ajinomoto seasoning plant. The factory, run by the former Union Ajinomoto Inc. and later Ajinomoto Philippines, had been producing seasoning on this Ugong site since 1962. It ran for about forty-five years before operations were shut down in October 2007. The shuttered plant was then transferred to a real estate affiliate, Union Ajinomoto Realty Corporation, which was later renamed Woodside Greentown Properties Inc.

Megaworld Corporation entered the picture in August 2013, when it acquired 100 percent ownership of Woodside Greentown Properties, including the old Ajinomoto land in Pasig. In March 2014, the company announced the project as its seventh township in Metro Manila and ninth nationwide, with a budget pegged at around 35 billion pesos over a ten-year build-out. At launch it carried the working name Woodside City, a nod to the holding company that owned the land. In September 2015, Megaworld rebranded the project to its current name, Arcovia City.

The public-facing parts of the township came online from 2019 onward. Arcovia Parade, the retail strip facing C-5, opened in early 2019, and that same February the Arco de Emperador archway was unveiled at the main entrance. Residential delivery followed, with the 37-storey 18 Avenue de Triomphe topping out in 2025. On April 1, 2024, 123,837 square meters of land along E. Rodriguez Jr. Avenue were proclaimed a Special Economic Zone and Information Technology Park, formalizing the township's role as a Pasig BPO and office hub.

place_intro:
Evia Lifestyle Center is a large open-air retail complex along Daang Hari Road in Las Pinas, at the southern edge of Metro Manila where the city border meets Bacoor, Cavite. It is the commercial anchor of Vista Alabang, the Villar family township that stretches across Las Pinas and parts of Muntinlupa, and it sits at the junction of Daang Hari and the Muntinlupa-Cavite Expressway (MCX).

The layout is not a single boxed-in mall. Buildings A and B run three floors including a basement, Building C goes up to four floors, and the North Wing climbs to six floors with two basement parking levels. The buildings are stitched together by a covered passageway and a wide atrium topped with a clear-glass roof, so daylight pours into the walkways. Total retail footprint is around 120,000 square meters.

The sub-areas locals name when meeting up here are the AllHome side, the All Day Supermarket wing, the cinema block with the IMAX with Laser screen, and the Coffee Project plus Fully Booked corner that anchors the cafe and bookstore zone. The crowd is mostly residents from Las Pinas, Paranaque, Muntinlupa, Laguna, and Cavite, plus MCX commuters passing through.

history.heading: Vista Land's southern anchor along Daang Hari
history.body:
Evia Lifestyle Center opened in 2012 as the retail centerpiece of Vista Alabang, the township project of the Villar-controlled Vista Land group. It is owned and operated by Vista Malls, the mall arm of Vista Land, and was planted on Daang Hari Road specifically to serve the residential subdivisions the company had been rolling out across Las Pinas, Muntinlupa, and the Cavite side of the border.

The name itself is a clue to the intent behind the site. Evia is short for Pontevia, meaning bridgeway, a reference to the position of the property at what Vista Land treats as the connecting point of the different parcels that make up Vista Alabang. The location at the junction of Daang Hari and the Muntinlupa-Cavite Expressway was picked because both roads were drawing new traffic from Cavite commuters heading into Metro Manila, which gave the center a built-in catchment from day one.

The complex was expanded in stages after the 2012 opening. The MX4D cinema was added on February 10, 2016, and the IMAX with Laser auditorium followed on November 19, 2018. At the time it opened, the IMAX with Laser screen here was the first of its kind in Southeast Asia, which is the detail most often cited when locals explain why the cinema wing draws people from outside Las Pinas. The North Wing, with its six floors and two basement parking levels, was part of the later build-out that turned the property from a single retail strip into the multi-building complex it is today.

place_intro:
Tiendesitas sits at the corner of Ortigas Avenue Extension and E. Rodriguez Jr. Avenue (C-5) in Barangay Ugong, Pasig City. It is part of the wider Ortigas East estate (previously branded as Frontera Verde), which covers roughly 18.5 hectares on the Pasig side of the Ortigas Center business district. From EDSA, it is the open-air shopping village on the right side of C-5 before the bridge toward Eastwood.

The complex itself covers about 30,000 square metres and is laid out as a cluster of low-rise pavilions instead of a single enclosed mall. There are twelve pavilions in total, organised into nine themed villages: Handicrafts, Fashion, Personal Care and Novelties, Pets, Garden, Antiques, Furniture, Food, and Delicacies. The roofs use chemically treated cogon grass, and the design borrows from Maranao motifs that trace back to the bahay kubo.

The character of the place is somewhere between a covered tiangge and a lifestyle park. Parking lots wrap around the pavilions, duyans hang in the walkways, and there is open ground between buildings for tents, car shows, and weekend bazaars. Jeepneys along C-5 drop you near the gate, and city buses stop a short walk away.

history.heading: From Frontera Verde to a Pasig shopping village
history.body:
Tiendesitas opened on September 26, 2005 as a project of Ortigas & Company Limited Partnership (OCLP), the same firm that has held the Hacienda de Mandaloyon estate since Don Francisco Ortigas and Dr. Frank W. Dudley acquired it from the Augustinian friars on January 20, 1920. The land it stands on was part of an 18.5-hectare interim development called Frontera Verde, an Ortigas Center expansion area on the Pasig side of C-5. Construction was reported at around P200 million.

The brief from OCLP was to build a permanent home for the kind of small Filipino traders who had been operating out of weekend tiangges and roadside stalls. The developer modelled the layout on a traditional tindahan village, commissioned twelve pavilions in a stylised Maranao silhouette, and grouped tenants by trade into the nine named villages still in use today. More than 450 traders from Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao formed the original merchant base, with handicrafts, antiques, furniture, plants, pets, and regional food products as the core categories.

The opening week in late September 2005 was anchored by a bonsai exhibit at the People’s Village, followed by an antique exhibit in October. Over the following years, Ortigas Land rebranded the surrounding estate from Frontera Verde to Ortigas East, with Tiendesitas positioned as its anchor retail destination. A later air-conditioned shopping building was added to expand the original open-air footprint, but the village layout, cogon-roof pavilions, and trade-based zoning that launched in 2005 remain the defining frame of the place.
